properties.Segment Text: "I don't know if I have a lot of stories but I'm very happy to help because I was also kind of part of the... Come, we can walk. Okay, let's start. It's noisy here because of the roadworks. I was also kind of part of the K37, not on the paper but... Swee Kang being my best friend from secondary school."
"When she first told me about the tip-off, I was like, what the heck? We have to do something. So I feel emotionally part of the project even though I could not get formally on the team."

"I'm happy that we got the school conserved although I told Laura, Swee Kang and I joined in the secondary school. So I think our first block was the other one... this was our last block in secondary school, the Lee Kong Chian block."
"We never studied in the beautiful primary school... it's the whole vibes and the architecture that's there... I probably went into that building maybe once or twice in my four years here."
"I believe that is going to be the only block that's conserved... the other blocks are not pretty."
"Actually, I'm very happy to tell you that according to the conservation plans, three blocks will be conserved... total three blocks."
"This hill, no one will forget because this is PE... everyone hated the hill... Hill of Joy, the Hill of Hell."
"So that is my memory of this part."
"This was my Sec 3 and Sec 4 block... This part was where we had our recess and all that... meaningful and familiar to me."
"The blue is not SEGS... ours is round and with the yin yang, which is half jade and half gold."
"I tried to pull out whatever memorabilia I could find... I should have my school badge somewhere."
"I graduated the year it was the 90th anniversary... that's what the school badge looks like... sky blue sleeveless beautiful uniform."
"This was the souvenir... something that I wrote for the school... I was 16."
"She did a lot for us growing up."
"This campus is only my second time in this... my mom studied here."
"PE classes... netball... basketball... short sojourn in the basketball team."
"This was our recess spot... classrooms upstairs... I would like to go upstairs."
"They've been having sporadic art exhibitions... I brought my son once."
"This feels like it would have been our classrooms... I wouldn't be able to recognise which one it was."
"These condos didn't exist... the view would have been completely different."
"I'm happy that it's conserved because it would have been really sad if it had torn down and become a condo."
"It looks a bit like fallen into disrepair... you kind of hoped..."
"Imagining it becomes a green, lung-ish oasis... but comes the question of maintenance."
"Everything is covered... I do not recall there being a cover."
"I'll never forget the lab... this was my secondary one lab!"
"Schoolgirls playing five stones... waiting for buses."
"These are the original bits... but the doors have changed."
"This is my Sec 1 block... original floor."
"Assemblies were here... flag raising, singing the school song."
"It looks gulaggy now with fences... but it was really pretty when it was open."
"The canteen is very much the heart of a school."
"I didn't spend a lot of time in the canteen... I brought my lunchbox every day."
"Principal's house... I didn't even know it was the principal's house."
"I've loved Emerald Hill and Cairnhill ever since... architecture."
"I love pre-war conserved houses... maybe one day we'll try to find something in Emerald Hill."
"This fancy block was the primary school... my life was that end."
"I'm a little torn... all-girls school versus mixed schools."
"This was where buses would stop... this looks original."
"URA conservation laws... sometimes applying the letter of the law creates something not usable."
"My first home was a pre-war shop house... we lived there over 20 years."
"Conservation can be very tricky."
"This driveway... kids running around, buses coming, super bustling."
"Peranakan Place... the craziest, most beautiful shop houses."
"DJ made jokes with us... I was embarrassed."
"The principal complained officially... super protective of us."
"Centrepoint kids... original punks... ban on going there."
"That orange La Taverna house... iconic Emerald Hill house."
"One Price Antique Store... never opened... wonder who owned it."
"During COVID I got stuck here... three years in Cairnhill."
"Hans Hofer store... travel guides... never forgot that."
"This was very significant for us walking up."
"They retained that stretch... DJ heckling... got rid of him."
"This tree has been here forever... everyone hanging around."
"Mini reunion... took a photo."
"This driveway was bustling... skipping rope, buses."
"The pubs were not here when you were in school."
"Walking back up... rain... noise."
"This has been the same since I was in school."
"I love walking around nowadays for work."
"This feels like walking up to this part because this is the same."
"Let me just stop this recording then."

"This is the entrance where the students will go in. This is just the main entrance, but normally the students will go in by this entrance. When was the last time you were back on this campus? I came back last two years ago when they had a restorative walk. To bring my daughter to come and have a walk. She's currently back to SCGS."

"This is the foyer before you get to the canteen. It's a very small canteen, probably about six stalls, that service both the primary and secondary schools. That corner is actually the famous mee pot stall. This is a very small canteen housing all the students. Typically you won't get as many seats as you do. What will you do if you do not? I'll skip recess. Skip recess? Too much effort."

"This is the green field. A lot of my classmates were saying that it's very small now compared to last time. It's probably because of the proportion of buildings next to it as well. Previously when we were at the school, you could generally see blue skies around you. You would feel that the field was very big. I mean it's not very big, but it's now caged up. This cage is actually add-on. Previously it was a big tree in front of the house giving a bit of an entrance portal."
"This is a big green field. The kids would play hot-scotch here. This is the lower primary. The upper primary will hang out around there."

"Do you feel very nostalgic being back here? It's alright. I only spent a good part of lower primary before the school moved to Dunedin High. So you were on both campuses? Yes, but we moved on to the second grade."

"This is the primary school block. This drain is where the primary 1 or 2 would kneel down here and learn to brush their teeth. You can imagine that there's a 40-student class. The teacher wouldn't bring you to the toilet and brush. They would kneel down there and the teacher would give you the toothbrush and teach you to brush your teeth. So that was a class for that."

"It was a kind of social studies thing. This one is the typical windows. The windows last time was green or something. This is probably updated based on the international... I think if this building is going to be conserved, they need to restore this window. So these windows are the traditional part of the original school design, but they have different colours."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): professional reflection (0.48), concern for preservation (0.41), attachment (0.34)
properties.emotion_all: Professional Reflection, Concern For Preservation, Attachment

properties.Segment Text: "Behind you is actually the famous bookshop. Now even the primary school bookshop is the most popular corner. It sells all the rubbish that you can think of with all the textbooks and all that. This is the corner of the bookshop and gym."
"So you're going up the stairs, it's actually the gym. Actually, the gym is actually a kind of mix because you can feel the bounce. It's not really made for it."

"Okay, so there's a lot of things that changed in the school. This one is actually now a toilet, but previously this is the teacher's office. So everybody who wants to get out of this corner of the school to go recess has to bypass the teacher. And the teacher put a mirror there so when she was facing that side, she can see whoever walked past."

"So then these are the classrooms. The lower primaries are normally at the lower floors and the upper primaries are at the climb stairs. Previously, this is an exposed stairs. Obviously as an architect, I know it's a fire compartment, so they close it up. It's actually a wooden plank stairs with railing spalestries."

"So generally this is really more the administrative corners and this side is the classrooms. And because the primary one, primary two, primary three are likely to be at the lower levels, they actually had to do morning and afternoon class because there's too many children already. Yeah, no aircon. No aircon."
"So this corner, as you see, this narrow corridor, if the children forget to bring homework, they'll be standing outside. So you'll see all the naughty children outside and the principal walk past."

"Okay, so if I'm not wrong, okay, this corner, what the corner is this one? It's actually the dance studio. So this school actually prides on ballet. And there's only one teacher who's actually good at that."

"So I think they have made a lot of changes to the school compared to what we had last time, but general ward divisions are still the same. Still that tiny little room that can squeeze a lot of children."
"Not so much, but my family stays at Booker T-Mart, so we go to Centre Point, but not to Amber Hill."

"So as someone who was in both campuses, did you ever have a preference? Yeah, I mean if you were here, you would feel that there's an attachment to the space. Because it's so tight, right? Then everybody will see everybody."

"But in the new school, I mean when we first moved, everybody was very happy because you got air-con, you got bigger lab, bigger space. So when we actually moved the campus, it's actually a blessing."
"So this is the main entrance. And the downstairs is all the home economics, the labs residing."
"This corner, this is my sec one corner. I did the sec one here."
"So this is the hall. And I believe this is my sec one class. So we can look down and you can see the big green field."
"And then our principal used to be very fierce. I think her principal office is probably around there, but she would yell from that corner so that everybody would stop running."
"So this is my secondary school, sec one class. To be honest, I don't have experience upstairs because secondary one, then after that we move out."
"But the experience is nice because this corner you actually get to see the green field. And then you connect back to where you graduated."
"But you don't feel so claustrophobic because of the densification of the high rise. But I know it's unavoidable."
"So this school was kind of caged because international schools are concerned about safety."
"So the primary will have their own hall, which is very small. This is the secondary school hall."
"I think that's all I have. Is that helpful?"
"As an architect, that building, that corner, this portal. So this proportion of the tree building actually gives you the feel that you're coming back home."
"But because they need to densify the building so that they can accommodate more students, then you feel that this comes in, this comes in."
"So actually we skipped the back, but the back is a forbidden corner because there's an old house by the principal and a tree that we think is haunted."
"So the children at secondary school will go out to Centre Point to eat. Just don't get caught."
"This is the forbidden area. This is the principal's. There's an old principal who stays here."
"There used to be a very big tall tree here and it's kind of haunted-ish."
"So this is a courtyard. Some of the upper primary will play basketball here."
"So was it easy to kind of go between blocks if it was raining? Within the blocks is fine."
"Would you like to take a look at the house? We went in before, but not during the school days, nobody dares to go in."
"So this is the old principal house. I've never seen a house like it."
"It's like a miniature of it, so she's living in a school with a miniature school on it. Always watching over."
"So that elevation and this elevation, this portal is very nice. It's quite iconic."
"So to think that it was a house and if you actually grew up in this house, it's quite iconic."
"I don't think they retain all the soft scapes, so the soft scapes are all new-ish."
"So my mum and my aunt all study from this school. So we are three, now with my daughter is the third generation."
"So we just have to be in the school. No choice. Just take it."
"The nice thing about this particular school is because it's also primary and secondary, we are in the same campus. So everybody know everybody."
"So I think that's all. I'm just going to take a quick picture."

"And they were all in Marine Parade and all gone. Yeah. So, what used to be Jelari, gone. Yeah, you can barely find the branch here or there. But I'll walk you down to Magnolia Snack Bar. Okay. If you don't mind. But you've got stuff up here still, yeah? Yeah, don't worry. Anyone else coming in or...? We'll have another interview after this. Okay, sorry. Let me know if you want me to cut it short. I'll just make it fast. No, of course, we're happy to go with you. Probably another five minutes. Oh, are they there already? No, no. Okay."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): loss / grief (0.55), nostalgia (0.41), mild anxiety (0.18), accommodation / consideration (0.22)

properties.Segment Text: "My next interview is with my mum, actually, because she was studying here. She was here too? Yes. When did she graduate? I cannot remember, but she's 60 this year. So... She's three years younger than I am. I'll be 63 this year. Yeah, so three years is my journey. Oh, wow. Okay. Yeah, but she was also here. You see, it's people who have been here who will remember."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): attachment / belonging (0.52), nostalgia (0.44), pride (0.31), continuity across generations (0.29)
properties.emotion_all: Belonging, Nostalgia, Pride, Continuity Across Generations

properties.Segment Text: "We'll just pop down. I'll just do this really quick for you. This time, you look fantastic for your age. I just saw my hairdresser. I wouldn't have guessed. Oh, I just came from a hairdresser last Saturday. And he touched up all my whites. Okay. Yeah, try not to grow too fat. If I grow fat, I lose weight. Grow fat, lose weight. Grow fat, lose weight. We'll get there."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): amusement (0.49), comfort (0.37), light self-awareness (0.28), social ease (0.34)
properties.emotion_all: Amusement, Comfort, Light Self-Awareness, Social Ease

properties.Segment Text: "All these shops here at that point in time were just houses. They weren't shops. This only came in much later. I think on that side, where the green building is now, towards the end, we had a mama shop. And that's where we would buy the stick bubble. The little balloon thingy, you fold it on the straw and you blow a bubble. I used to play with that. Yes. It used to be there."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): nostalgia (0.82), childhood joy (0.58), attachment to place (0.44), wistfulness (0.36)
properties.emotion_all: Nostalgia, Childhood Joy, Attachment To Place, Wistfulness

properties.Segment Text: "And that's the only thing I would buy there. I didn't buy anything else because I didn't need anything else. And I wasn't really a sweet person. Oh, yes, I did. It's those little small tube candy. It was like sour plum. Okay."
"But, yeah, all this is totally new. So, I don't know this part of it anymore."

"So it used to be much quieter when you were a kid. Oh, much. And this was the road. That comes to drive up and down. More down, it's one way. Yeah. Opposite used to be OCBC. Yeah. OCBC Bank. Yeah. And that's where we had... And in between, they had a hotel, Hotel Phoenix. That was there as well. But what is here... Before Center Point."

"Sorry, just a little section here. Yeah. We had what we called the Magnolias Neck Bar. And they had only two. One was here. It's somewhat attached to cold storage. And the second one was actually in the library, near... NLB, is it? Near the NLB, but actually, NPH Bookstore. And it's called the NLB Bookstore. At the NLB."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): place attachment (0.47), recall / cognitive effort (0.33), nostalgia (0.39)

properties.Segment Text: "Yeah, that was the second Magnolias Neck Bar. So when this one closed, we had that one. And they renovated that area. And it was Neck Bar. That was it."
"Yeah. But yeah, this is my memory. And like I said, this was our set four. In grain memory. Yeah. We always come here, study, and we always order set lunch."
"Set lunch always came with a soup. A little dry piece of bread. The main meal. And a scoop of ice cream. And tea or coffee."
"But after we ate, it was time to go home. We never studied. No studying done. Yeah. I know there was 30 minutes studying and then we're going home now."
"Okay. Yeah. Thank you so much for sharing your memories. I think we are now out of time. So I will stop the recording."
"This is just like where everyone started falling actually. When they first built it up, it was just cemented. And everyone who ran here kept falling."
"That canteen is a new one and we never had a cage. Never, never, ever. I think that came out with Chatsworth International."
"The original site would be that one. That's the main building, primary school."
"LKC is what we call the secondary block. But this is technically the new canteen. I didn't eat here."
"When I was here, the canteen was somewhat... we didn't have an upper level at that point in time. And my favourite stall was the drink stall."
"My favourite vantage point during recess was this one... These are the steps to nowhere, literally."
"This was the gathering point for many of us in secondary school."
"Sports hall was badminton hall... but this would be my classroom. I was here for 10 years."
"This was 1A... I was in 1B. That was my classroom."
"I don't really remember much. I only remember little spots."
"She said that I was her first friend... which I don't remember."
"When I joined the CJS as a teacher, she was still here... she just saw all of us grow up."
"We had stamps... deposit the money into the post office savings bank. Very archaic, very traditional."
"This here was our then one and only air-conditioned room, the music room."
"I joined SCGIS in 1970... primary one."
"The lack of sequence... you jump from primary one to primary four."
"This was a very sought after classroom because of the door... the principal would walk through."
"It just got rid of the boredom of lessons."
"Mrs Long was somewhat eccentric... punishments such that... make us strip and stand facing the wall."
"The washroom... smelly. So I never ever wanted to go."
"This was primary 5 and 6... the wall was never here."
"This was the office... general office, bursar, registrar."
"The hall... assembly... framed photos of the Queen."
"This was the bookshop... prime space."
"We were always told not to go into the road. Dangerous."
"The principal's house... seemed to be haunted."
"This entire section never used to be here... 5BX exercises."
"The haunted tree... but we loved playing anyway."
"It was used as an art room because we ran out of classrooms."
"We had a well... and banana tree... hauntings again."
"The warden lived here... two dogs, Blackie and Brownie."
"The cabbage patch... keep off the grass."
"We used to line up and brush teeth here. It was a campaign."
"This place is limited in space... but we maximise."
"I hated reading... so I became a librarian."
"This was our little library... nobody came up here."
"This is not part of the conservation site."
"Waiting for my father at the corner... primary school."
"No shelter. Rain or shine."
"Magnolia Snack Bar... our haunt."
"The Kacang Kut Tei man... quiet, elderly."
"He went back to India... that was the end."
"Campus-wise, these are my main memories."

"So typically we say, imagine that you're arriving to the school. How would you go around it? How would you use the space? And then towards the end, how would you leave the school? And then we will follow you out. Like a day in the life. Yeah. Maybe we should start at the beginning in France. Sure, we can do that. Let's take a little just couple of steps up first. What did you first notice when you walked through the space?"

"You see, the Emerald Hill Roadside in the old days, of course, this building only came up much later. But you still could never drive into the school grounds, although there was a little driveway and a gate. So even if you could, you drove in from the other side, the Kent Hill Roadside, but it was only for teachers. And in those days, not many teachers drove, so we had only a little car park. So it was just pure impossible for buses or parents' cars to try and come in."
"So it was just like you just go quickly up that little slope, you drop your kids off, and then you went out. So I'll show you what I mean when we get there."

"Oh, it was just an empty patch of land. Like grass? Yeah, a bit of grass and some trees. It was a hill. It has always been like this in terms of the gradient. And down below here, where this little road space is, used to be a little Malay kampong. A couple of Malay houses, you know? Wheeled wooden... Like the atap houses."
"In those days, we never thought of safety or whatever. You know, we just thought they were our neighbours."
"This was still here, this patch of green, which we used to call the cabbage patch because it was so small. We did everything here."

"Just a bit of orientation before we begin. Because this is the original building in that sense. This is the 1924 building. And outside there, you'll see another bungalow, which is a later addition, and was meant to house the principal and her family."
"So everything else, all the other blocks, came up later. This block on my right here, we named it the Song Ong Siam block after one of our co-founders."
"We had an old little... again... like... a kind of a... one-story thing that used to be our canteen. And this, later on, was redeveloped."
"Previously, this was the quarters for the servants. We had servants' quarters. Yes, our servants used to live in. We had a gardener. We had two gardeners."
"Because don't forget, this originally was a property called Clare Grove that belonged to Lim Boon King."
"I still remember, as a young girl, we walked up and down, and there weren't all these apartments, of course. Now we are totally surrounded by them."
"I lived at actually the other end of Orchard Road, near Changlin Orange Road. I could walk here, it was maybe a bit more than one kilometre."
"And in those days, Orchard Road was a two-way street. So you could go home by just crossing this road here."
"We didn't have that many stalls. Maybe we had four main stalls, but the noodle stall here is iconic."
"In those days, a bowl of kueh chia was like 30 cents. Unheard of now."
"This building was what we call the principal's bungalow. It's a very pretty, rather unique house, so to speak."
"In my primary school days, I think we were never allowed to come here. To enter the house, certainly we were not allowed."
"We used to play around it and imagine there were ghosts there as children."
"Unfortunately, the tree didn't fall on this side. It would have hit this house."
"This was an area where the students would play and come and spend their recess time."
"But when we wanted to lay the concrete for the basketball court... we had no choice but to cut down that tree. It was a real pity."
"The whole atmosphere is not the same. No, it's not the same."
"I saw a lot of broken pieces of glass on the ground... later in the day we heard the news that the Israeli embassy... had been bombed."
"We had barely standing rooms. Especially on a rainy day, they all had to be crammed under the canteen."
"I remember this classroom. We had a primary school teacher... Mrs. Molly Nunes. She was always very kind, very nice, and we really loved her."
"When the international schools came in, they glassed up everything, they air-conditioned the buildings."
"We didn't have so much concrete here. It was all still grass and there were trees growing here. So the place was definitely more green."
"Before we left, we had a big carnival, big fair, and a so-called sale of old school stuff."
"Usually in those days, I guess, you only went into the principal's office if you were about to get a reprimand."
"She really dedicated herself to school. Her whole life was here."
"So when we were here, we would conduct our morning assembly flag raising here."
"This was a popular recess time place to, as usual, you know, girl chatter here."
"This was the exam room. We had all our big exams here, including our O-levels."
"The concert thing has always been a very traditional SCGS activity."
"Being an all-girls school... our needs are a bit different."
"Girls being girls, somehow, you know the way they somehow cling more to each other."

"We didn't have the luxury of being able to give our teachers a separate little cubicle to themselves as we now can in the new school. We also had morning and afternoon sessions. All the teachers would sit around on a huge table or if you were lucky, had one little desk at the edge of a corner. During the morning and afternoon school, they had to make sure they had cleared enough space, done their work sufficiently so that when the afternoon teachers came in, the afternoon teachers can then take over. So in that sense, the sense of belonging, that understanding that we really are sharing space and we really are working together. I think the camaraderie was much closer. I often saw them eat food together, bring makan or tiffettes and share."
"They still do nowadays but they're more busy and the school day being longer. In those days, you had no choice. One o'clock means you pack up and go because the next session is coming in."

"As I told you, even the kids themselves sort of knew where to farm their spaces up and where to play games even on a rainy day when there was practically almost no shelter. So the same classroom is downstairs and upstairs. Identical, so again, all the way, whatever classrooms there were. We never had this overhead thing. I don't know what this structure is. Like a shelter, I think they covered it up, I guess."
"Okay, we can head to outside. Okay, I mean, are you happy with... Yes, it's great. Thank you so much. Learning so much about the school. Of course. You're most welcome. My pleasure."
"So far, we've only had mostly students coming in so it's nice to hear a different perspective on how the space is."
"Up to the year before when we did the KEEK 37, Emerald Hill Road thing, we hadn't been in here for a long, long, long, long time. Because coming here was a bit of a problem."

"At least during the time when the foreign schools were here, we could easily just ask principal. But after that, when URA took over, or land authority now, I think, you know, you have to go through more bureau to see."

"So many favourite spots. I loved that front garden because, you know... Especially the big staircase that led down to the so-called the garden. And I forgot to tell you, in the middle of that garden was a well."

"Because this was an old house, I guess they had no fresh water in that sense. From the tap, you know, no tap water. So water must have been drawn from that well. But by then, the well was not open. It had already been covered up by huge slabs of granite to prevent anybody from falling in."
"That was one of my favourite spots. Of course, I saw this building coming up. I enjoyed the cookery lessons there."

"And there was one incident when... we were not using electric stoves. We were using gas stoves. And you know, even the ovens, you have to sort of put in a matchstick or whatever to light up. And there was this silly girl who I think had put her head in a little too close as she lighted. And boom, I think she got a bit singed."
"So that's one incident. But we've had a few, of course, before accidents."
"Look at all these tall buildings around here. No, but that makes this place even more special. There's so much contrast. We're even in the heart of Orchard."
"We were always contemplating what is the future of this site going to be. We were always afraid they would tear it down."

"That's why when it occurred to the alumni a couple of years back that, no, this had not been officially conserved. They really worked towards ensuring that it would be kept, at least the site and some of the buildings."
"I had a number of friends who lived along this road. As I always said to people, you talk about neighborhood schools. We were a neighborhood school."
"In the past, of course, hawkers were allowed to ply the streets. After school, we could come out and eat char kway teow."

"These are like 1928 buildings. They are conserved houses like the Blair Road and Koon Seng Road houses. But beyond the end of these two rows of Peranakan houses in those days, were huge properties, big mansions."
"They were really huge. Exactly as House Telecon described it."
"I've done a few tours for people, taking them up the road and walking them through and explaining them about the design and architecture and the windows and the carvings that you see."
"But also, you see, this is something very Peranakan, the two half doors, or what we call the pintu pagar."
"And some are beautiful in design and decoration and carving."
"Where, when you turn right, that section, up to the corner of Cairnhill Road, used to be a beautiful stretch of three-storey Peranakan houses. They were gorgeous."
"That's where the fabled house is at the other end... That whole road, that whole road. Yeah, it's all gone."
"Nothing we can do, right, in the name of progress."
"This frontage has not changed because it is a very old building. It has not changed because it is not allowed to change."
"We constantly had to re-envision things and to find out how much more of our past and our history and how then we could capitalize on its value, the intrinsics."
"So our school colours are jade and gold... represents qualities that we want in women. Their resilience, their unchanging quality, their strength."
"It's embedded in our badge. It's embedded in our school song. In all that we do."
"We never allow them to forget that they are an SCGS Kim Gek."
"You can be tough, you can be firm, but you must not lose your femininity."
"It's true also, but you see because we were an independent school right from the start... we were very poor for a long time."
"Today's schools... how much the MOE puts into them. But of course they may lack a certain amount of autonomy."
"When we built SCGS in Dunearn Road, we had to raise extra money to ensure that we didn't just have plain concrete floors."
"At first I was a little bit hesitant when they showed us the design and said the roof would be this kind of green."
"So in the end it turned out well."
"Now that there are possibilities that a school or a place for education is going to turn into a place of commercialisation, how do you feel?"
"I like to always see positive things, especially in the future."
"We really have to depend on people with very deep pockets... they will not understand the history."
"I always believe that if that old school building could be turned into THE Peranakan Museum... That is my dream, my personal dream."
"You will need to revitalise the space... all your cultural amenities."
"I would love to see the old building really become THE Peranakan Museum. It's a perfect setting."
"People could then perhaps continue to capture the spirit of the Peranakan heritage."
"My mother-in-law used to go out and play Chukky every day."
"Thank you so much... come back to me anytime."

"Okay, sure. Um actually if you look down this corridor, this is probably the how most of us will come to school if we take a bus. So this is the entrance that comes in from Emerald Hill Road. Okay, there's a little staircase here as well on the right. Some parents will drop off their kids but this this walkway, at least for me, was how I came into SCGS on my first day of school in primary one. Okay. So most of you will come up this corridor and then just follow this walkway up to the canteen on the left and then the primary school on the right. Okay, maybe we'll take a pause there."

"So when did you matriculate, when did you join SCGS? I joined SCGS in primary one in 1984. I left SCGS from the secondary four in 1993. So I was the last batch to graduate from Emerald Hill. The following year the school moved over to its current Dunn and Road premise. So it's quite special for my badge and we wear it with a pride of honour that it's a badge of honour that we are the last batch to complete 10 years in the Emerald Hill campus."

"You can take a look at the canteen. Yes. Okay, so once you come in, most of us will hang out in the canteen and either wait for school to start. This on the left is the famous noodle store. Of SCGS. So if you speak to an old girl, everybody will tell you about the mee pok and the mee pok and this is the original mee pok store."

"There is still a version of the mee pok store in a new school. It's operated I think by the relatives of the original owner but this is where we queue up. This one will have the longest queue. And then it didn't look like this but the stores used to be laid out in a U-shape manner."
"So it's this, it goes in, U and then it comes out. So this whole area where that's horded is probably empty last time. So when you go in, you get your full choice of all the canteen stalls."

"Later on if you have access, if you go up, the gym is upstairs. You can take a move. I would then pause here because Amarok Hill is not a very big school. You just stand here, you can see pretty much the whole school."

"On your left, you can take a look at this building. This is the primary school block. This was donated to the school by one of the founders of the school, Mr Lim Boon King. He's a very prominent philanthropist and also educator."

"He was one of the founders of the school who felt that girls, particularly from the straight Chinese households or the Peranakan, should also have a good education. So they couldn't find a suitable location for the school."

"So when the Peranakan community wanted to scale up, the house of Mr Lim Boon King was used. That's also one of the unique reasons why this school is in this enclave because this whole area is the Peranakan residential area."

"So Amarok Hill and the TCS series Amarok Hill, it all came from this area. So you can imagine back in the old days, they would stay in the houses and then the daughters would come over here for school."
"Oh absolutely because most of the girls of the Peranakan community, if they could go to school, they would probably be sent here."

"So this door, okay if you take a look at this building, and I think it means more architecturally to you guys than us, it's a symmetrical building. So there are two wings, a wing on the left, a wing on the right, and then this frontal area in the middle."

"Okay this middle area where you are, if you zoom up that middle room right under the triangle, is the principal's office. So technically she can pop her head out of that window and scold girls who's making a lot of noise playing games downstairs during canteen."

"She will also scold the old girls for running on the grass because back then there was no astroturf, it was just grass and we can't run on the grass because then the grass will die so we always cannot go on the grass."
"But all these hard floors are quite important. They're where the girls will play games during recess."
"So they'll play five stones, they'll play zero point, they'll play catching, they'll play all sorts of games that is in fact back in those days."
"How many girls will be playing at one time? It's quite small but when you're a primary school girl this is very big."
"I was just telling my sister actually I remember this being much bigger because it's like a place like this."

"Okay this if I may I think this is the building that earned us the conservation status because okay when I was young I used to always imagine it being someone's house because it was someone's house."
"So if you go in you can imagine the family will come in and every one of this will be a room."
"Okay but we made it into different purposes so unfortunately this toilet used to be our teacher's room."
"This is my primary one classroom."
"So when I was a kid I remember when school started very early this whole corridor was very dark."
"So when you come in you're walking around in like kind of darkness that's like a very small flickering light."
"That that was my impression of school early in the morning."
"I think this was the music room yes music room right yeah this was the music room."
"There is a piano here and there'll always be music here because the teachers are having lessons."
"So these are another typical classroom see there's always two doors."
"Fortunately they boxed up this area the staircase is not visible."
"Last time around this is the central concourse."
"In the past you would this whole thing will be open the whole door will be open and sunlight will come in."
"So when I was young and I was up to my fantasies I used to imagine oh what did people do in this house when it was a house."
"This class every morning in the first or second class may be disrupted because if that day the principal goes out for meeting."
"So I always remember this classroom being quite."
"So the rest of the building is the same layout pretty much exactly the same mirror image."
"I kind of like it because I think it's like like a house right it's a mirror image."
"So this place on the left would be the principal's office."
"She gets a bird's eye view of the entire school from here."
"So you have to remember all old schools have no aircon so everything will rely on natural ventilation."
"We used to have louver windows."
"But that's part of those things."
"Okay okay that's the primary school block but the hall oh the hall is very important."
"We had assemblies in the hall we sing national anthems in the hall."
"The dancers dance in the hall."
"So it's a very very small type school."
"So if the principal wants to give a assembly speech she'll do the same."
"So we will all sit all the way back all the girls will line up according to their classes."
"Back then it's like somebody's house."
"This is also so interesting that they chose a student to play the piano."
"Okay there's a hidden place here."
"This is the auditorium it's very small but back in our time it was a very precious auditorium."
"If you ask me for my memories this field has a lot of memory."
"For all major festive celebrations we come together as a school."
"So to me this this central square design was quite an important experiential point."
"That's why I think sgs tend to be quite family oriented."
"In the bigger school of course I think that's something that you probably will."
"So this place yeah what's our favorite place because it was the bookshop."
"You take your precious 20 cents to come and buy some useless eraser useless pen."
"This is a big black hole of allowance money."
"So again you can imagine recess time the girls will all be playing."
"There was a teeth brushing program."
"So this building in front here is the principal's house."
"They sometimes call it miss tan's house or the art room."
"This swimming pool was not there."
"So actually there was a lot of stairs to climb."
"So this is orchard road can you believe it right."
"Our old principal used to be very strict about us loitering after school."
"When you were young did you ever feel unsafe no no you had a lot of fun."
"This was the back entrance of the school."
"When i was young being in this block means you're the big fish in the pond."
"This is my sec four classroom."
"It still looks exactly the same."
"So it's amazing that as you age and grow up you get to be around more spaces on the campus."
"I used to like the sound of water gushing out."
"This is where they teach you life cycle of the frog."
"Parents can come quite easily."
"So this courtyard is the secondary school courtyard."
"We had a flag lowering last time."
"Unless it rains then everybody very happy then you don't need to sing."
"So this little nook here yeah it's quite special because this is how everybody gets out of school."
"The old schools all have corridors it's very important for ventilation."
"So again like i said you will stand here and you look down."
"And you can see everything that's happening."
"The whole campus circles around the field."
"So we walk the campus and you can see different views of the field the whole time."
"That's it oh beautiful."

properties.Text: "Okay. When we come into school, we're not allowed to use that main gate. Partly because it's within the school building, and we'll be disturbing the secondary school students. Since we're primary school students, we're always entered by this gate. The story of why I got into SCGS is also here because, back in those days, this would have been the late 60s, early 70s, your parents were actually getting into school by just queuing up. Oh, really? Yeah, I mean, now it's impossible. And SCGS is like, if your mother wasn't even an old girl, don't even try. But my mother was in Shih Tzu Fairfield. My parents probably expected me to enrol there. But the story that my dad tells is that he was passing by Orchard Road and up Runway, and he found a queue here, and his friend was in the queue. And he asked, why are you queuing for SCGS? And the friend told him about it. Then, on the spur of the moment, he went, it sounds like a good school. And actually, my cousin, who was two years older than me, was already in the school. Then he figured, then my spell, try his luck, queue, send his daughter in here, because he had the school's group. And then I would have someone I know already in there, because she's my cousin. Which is the story of how I got into the school. Wow. This gate. So they would physically queue at this gate to register? Yes. Wow. It's amazing. Because I think in those days, probably the primary one cohort was a bit small, and you could do that sort of thing. Now, it's impossible. No way. It's much harder."

"So when you're coming into the school, this was the gate you used, and then you go right into the field, and to the left was the canteen. But I know just block is new. It wasn't here when I was in the school. The canteen at that time was just like a shed, really. So it didn't have this structure on top of it? I had never been into this building before, but I think they built it maybe the year that I was leaving. I left in 1980. I know it's like a fancy hall upstairs, and there's a dance studio because GGS was known for dance and all that. But I've never been there."

"So this is the primary school block, and next to it would have been the principal's house, which nobody dared to go near. There used to be a big tree in front of it. The principal's house was like where you're supposed to be quiet, and not disturbed, peace and all that. So this whole area, we girls kind of avoided it. So we'd usually be hanging around there."

"Most of us came by bus from Emerald Hill Road. If you came in by Cain Hill, you were considered upper-class because your family lives in District 9 in Cain Hill. The only time we probably came in around this area was like recess because the canteen's next door. And then you go straight to the playing basketball course and everything down there."

"I always felt like... I don't know how she felt living in a school. Right? Because you're living where you work. That would be very weird for most of us today. I'm trying to imagine this as a school, but I can't. Because I think of it as a school. But in a home, I suppose this would be her living room and then her bedroom and stuff. It's quite big, no? It's very cosy. And quite secluded, I guess? Because you won't be in the middle of Orchard Road because the trees are blocking all the sound. And I suppose this is a very rare kind of pocket, right? Within the Orchard Road area. You won't get this. I imagine she would have been living here rent-free as well. Oh my god, that's a good gig, man."

"There used to be a huge tree here. The tree was all like the demarket, but you don't go beyond the tree and disturb this building. The basketball court would be where we played, even under the hot sun. And then you see the slope of the grass there. That was off-limits. Because you couldn't climb the slope because they were afraid that you would fall and hurt yourself. And then there's a ledge there, right? So I think the really like, gung-ho girls would like walk up that edge and see how far you can go. Before somebody, some teacher saw you and stuck her head out on the window and screamed at you."

"Because these walls weren't here, the two staircases were facing each other. One was up and one was down. When you entered, it would have been like the middle of the building. It was an open foyer and then you had stairs coming down both sides. It wasn't like a grand staircase, but it was a milder version of that. So you could tell you were coming into a foyer staircase, going up to some rich guys, you know, big house and there are rooms upstairs and there are obviously rooms downstairs. So now that it's all walled up, I think you've lost that feeling that you enter into a grand foyer and the sweeping staircase really lifts your eye looking upwards. So the foyer would have been like double story and it was really nice and bright and airy. So I think all this has been lost."

"As a primary 1 kid, you would like run around a lot. Because that environment was wide and sweeping and expensive, so there's space. And when there's space as a kid, you run. And you play like catching games and running games and all that and basically annoy the teachers. You would ask permission to go to the toilet. And you didn't need to run, it wasn't urgent, but because there's this huge corridor that you just ran. I don't know, for the hell of it or something. There's a sense of freedom."

"As with all schools, there's always a rumor of the haunted toilet. They said the last cubicle was haunted. The other thing was apparently during the Japanese occupation, the Japanese occupied this building, but I don't know what they used it for. I don't think they killed or tortured people here. It's probably maybe like an administrative base or something. But then there's always the rumors, all the Japanese are here and they did that and then they ghosted and stuff like that."

"The school assembly meant that you would have 12 classes all lined up here and sitting here. I was just one of the things you did in school for assembly, la, once a week so no real feelings about it. It was just one huge big open room. If your class happened to be lining up at the back, you were told to be less noisy because this part obviously overloaded the principal's house."
"Then by the time you walk here, then you turn around here, you kind of like froze."

"I got locked in the toilet once. The cleaner was cleaning and then I wanted to go in and then she said no, no, no. I thought she meant no, no, no, don't use this cubicle because she's cleaning it. I didn't realise that she meant no, don't stay in the toilet at all. So I did my business, she shut, I heard the lock click and I went like, okay, die. It was recess time, I just heard banging on the door. I heard and then I said like, go find your teacher, go find anybody. She went to find the cleaner and the cleaner locked me. But I couldn't, I've gone like really bad."

"I tried so hard to be a librarian because I just wanted to sit in this room and be surrounded by books. I wasn't, but my friend was, so she let me in. It was one of my refuge as well. Not so much during recess, but let's say like after school and then after ECA, so you had time to kill. I spent a lot of time there. I think in those days, you would not have used the term a safe space, but that probably was to me because I'm very introverted. I didn't want to mix around too much. So that was a great place for me to just sit and read."

"You feel like you're on your own little world, and you're looking out at rooftops. It's kind of soothing now. Like you can look out at the wall and hide from it. I think a lot of the people who hung around the library were just the quiet, introverted reader type. So we like stuff like this. This is a lovely view."

"When you finished primary school and you got onto SAC 1, it was a big deal because you moved into that building facing the main road. I don't know. I think it was when you graduated from primary school, and you moved to another one."

"It's quite fun actually, just to see what is still standing. And I know that another school took over the premises and how they changed it and all that. So I'm not surprised that they modernised the windows and the classrooms and things like that. But I'm surprised that they bordered out that part where the staircase is in the primary school building. Because I thought that was so lovely."

"In the future if this site were to be redeveloped, what would you like to see that would make you come back here? I think the style of the whole property would be the primary school wing because it is very obviously a residential house. It is very unique and you don't see this sort of thing anymore. It seems to be from the era and from that style but obviously on a much bigger scale. I would like to see it redone up. If you took all that down and then do some alfresco stuff, I think it would be very nice especially in the middle of Orchard Road. It would be like a little really Alkaaf Mansion in the middle of Orchard Road. I would be sorry to see them go but then if it's actually added to a better set of property altogether, it would not be a bad idea I don't think. I would like to see something like that."
"I think it'd be nice to like meet your old friends, have a drink or two here. That's something. And definitely it's meaningful to be able to come back here with your peers."
"Because our school uniform, I don't know if you saw photos, but it used to be really like square neckline and it was this shade of blue."

"Really? I feel like ever since I was in Primary 1, which was in 2007, like the second we came into the school, that was just the identity of the students already. That's very interesting because I feel like a lot of... I would think that the kin geek identity was a very traditional one, but it wasn't. I only realized that because my friends' kids went, right? And then they started using that term."

"I only remember that there used to be some Italian restaurant across the road, but I don't think it's there. It's probably gone a long time. I had a friend who actually lived pretty halfway up the street in one of the houses. But I lost touch with her after secondary school. I don't even know if the family still owns it."

"Yeah I think I remember I usually walk in through this gate and then the canteen will be right there on the left. Yup. Are we allowed to approach? You're allowed to walk wherever as long as it's not locked. Well of course there's a lot of open space now. Last time there was fence all the way, just this narrow path to get into the canteen. Really? So I remember my primary one classroom is inside there, the corner one. You can go into the building. Okay so I can enter? Yeah. Okay so yeah definitely looks so different now. It was more a very open space previously and of course the most popular store is the mee pok stall."

"But in my younger days I would always pack food from home because recess time is just too short to queue up, buy food and eat and I've always been a slow eater. And naturally when it's our first year in primary school we'll have parents coming here and last time they didn't restrict you know not like these days where they restrict you parents would come every day. Oh yeah so when my mum is able she will come and buy food for me, she'll queue up first, she'll buy the fish ball noodle first, she'll wait for me."

"So what was this area? I don't remember this one. There were some people who said that this used to be a bookshop. But it has been kind of bordered over now. But my time the bookshop was inside the primary school. Oh okay so it's different. So I think a lot of things have changed."

"Well we did not have a lot of playing areas last time so mainly we either played here at the field or at the back where there's a basketball court. Primary school I remember just always playing here and we used to have all sorts of activities in this field. There was always like sports day then national day and I remember playing games here and dancing here as well."

"Okay this is new we never had this before. Oh okay this is new also. It's so different. I don't remember here being a toilet but I definitely remember this room. The first room was actually my classroom. So maybe that could have been my classroom."

"So last time there were only fans and there's no air condition and we have blackboards. That time it's just chalk and blackboards. We didn't have all the modern facilities that you now have like your markers and whiteboards. So we had wooden table and wooden chairs."
"I broke a fan upstairs. So we were without fans for a while and I was responsible."

"And at the end would be a toilet. Yep. Is it still there? Okay it's still here and mind you in those days it was only squatting. Oh yes. Okay it's all changed. Yeah okay we only had squatting. I don't remember sitting toilet post."
"I never came back after I graduated in 1982. But how does it feel being back? It's a nice feeling. I could only remember a bit not very much."

"There was a famous vice principal who was pretty fierce and when she's not happy with any of the students work she'll throw the exercise book out of the window. Yeah I remember witnessing it before."
"So there was of course happy times where you have lots of chit chatting in a classroom. And not so happy time when the teacher come and scold you and then you get punished."
"I hit the fan and one of the blade broke and it came down. So we were without fans for a while and I was responsible."

"Something unfortunate happened when we were in Song Ong Siam. During those days, we had a pair of sisters who were troublemakers. They created some trouble for another classmate. She was so upset that this classmate attempted suicide. She wanted to jump off the building. Because this was the highest floor. I think she was over there in that corner. Because I was in class and there was a lot of shouting like, don't jump, don't jump. So of course we all came out to see what was going on. And then I think the teachers also told us, don't stare, go back to your classroom. But I remember I think she was over there trying to jump. It was all some misunderstanding created by the two sisters. So it was very unfortunate. But glad she didn't jump."
"If this place were to be redeveloped, what would you like for it to become? Another educational place. I had fond memories of my education here. It was a foundation."

"Because SCGS is famous for dancing. So if a school could come in here and hold some dance classes, I think that would be great. This place could actually be used to teach people things. And I think people of all ages can come here, right? So because this one has got roots in Peranakan culture, maybe older folks such as myself could come here and learn Peranakan cooking and beading of shoes. And younger people could come here and learn hip-hop. Young ones maybe can come here and learn piano. You know, you could get some schools to come in and rent the spaces here and hold classes."
"We were learning about frogs... I ended up pulling out the whole tongue. Oh my god. So there was some veins dangling."
"We could still see the heart beating. So it was quite a horror site. I remember some girls screaming but I have never really been the screaming type."
"Yes, because it's very quiet and I just like being at the top of a building usually. Funny, I thought I can still smell the library. It feels as though I can still smell the books."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): nostalgia (0.92), comfort (0.83), attachment / belonging (0.77), sensory memory (0.88)

properties.Text: "We all had to brush our teeth after recess. So we were all squat in a row here, just beside the field, and we'd be brushing our teeth."
"Ms Tan was a very nice lady, very caring for all the students, very nice. I don't remember her raising her voice at all. First thing that she's a very soft-spoken lady."
"I feel so bad like I'm trespassing my old principal's house."
"I wish we had kept the old windows, but I guess they were really too old."
"I think that's all I can remember to share with you. Hope it's useful for your research."

"When I first walked here, I thought this whole space looked smaller than I thought. Perhaps when I first encountered this area was when I was a very young child, probably primary school. So now all these, the difference in level seems very insignificant to me, where it was very big and huge, I had to climb over. So coming down from those steps, that's where I parked my car beyond, I just thought, oh this space looks narrower than before."

"And an interesting story I like to share is the drain here, covered drain, they used to be open drain. So it is quite wide, I think it looks about 550cm. I recall a time when all the buses were parked along this street to take all the shuttle, all the school kids home. So there will be a mad rush when the bell rings, primary schoolers will be waiting for the secondary schoolers to come out, and there will be a final bell. So when the final bell rings, all the younger girls will rush into the bus because they want to get the better seats before the big teacher comes out. So there when the bell rang, I ran towards the bus, but I tripped and I fell headlong into this tree, specifically somewhere near the column there, probably tripped over the column. So I did a dive and I was really stuck, both hands inside, somehow I managed to do a handstand inside the drain. And my skirt slipped down, I was like, oh no, I wasn't very hurt. But I felt two hands hold my feet and pull me up, and that was the bus driver. So that incident really had an impact on me, and now I see the drain is covered, it's like, oh very good, no safety. So that's one of the stories I remember about open drains here."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): nostalgia (0.84), embarrassment (0.71), fear (0.55), relief (0.68), gratitude (0.63), amusement (0.52)
properties.emotion_all: Nostalgia, Embarrassment, Fear, Relief, Gratitude, Amusement

properties.Text: "And then I was mentioning about long ago that I did some more mischievous act where I trespassed into the school. At that time it was being transformed into the Chetworth International School. I think I was one of the, I was a more mischievous young adult at that time, about 20s. So a bunch of us after, I recall there was a football match, and we took the wrong bus. After we finished the match, we left the stadium, we took the wrong bus, and we ended up here in Orchard Road. And we were supposed to head towards the east. And then we saw this building, it was like the windows were all broken, they were in the middle of changing the frames. And so I think it was still timber window, timber frames. And it was like open, some of the paints were missing. And I think there were five of us, we climbed through over this sill and into the, but it was, I think it was a science lab at that time. So the tall benches were still there, the science table, and we went inside. And that was our, and we went to explore the inside. So maybe I can talk about where we went to explore and we went inside. Yeah, that would be fun. We went through the science lab."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): excitement (0.74), nostalgia (0.77), thrill (0.69), curiosity (0.66), mild anxiety (0.42)
properties.emotion_all: Excitement, Nostalgia, Thrill, Curiosity, Mild Anxiety

properties.Text: "Okay, so maybe previous fellow SCGS girls would have shared that after school, we still loitered around this area, because we needed to, those who took the private school bus would be sitting here to wait for the bigger girls to finish. And so we would be playing four stones, some of us would be jumping over that band or rubber band, you know, a string of rubber band, I think, we call it zero point. So I think many have fallen, there's still no railing down here. So many kids have fallen here. Wow, going through this like takes me back. It looks the same though, it's just very worn down here."
"This used to be the canteen, I'm sure it sticks off of what it was. Less stalls, there used to be a recess for more stalls, but there's always a crowd."
"I hope they don't demolish this place."

"So then this was grass, so they would always get muddy shoes because the ground would be kind of like a little bit muddy. And I remember there was a time when I was friends with two sisters, they kind of bullied me when I was younger. So they would put me, it was quite narrow this walkway, and just off to the left and on the right were, I think there was an exora bush here. Okay. So we all liked to pluck the flowers and then we'd sit for the night. But it was very muddy there too. So when we were walking, one of the sisters kind of pushed me into the bush and I got my shoes cracked. So we played a game, you know, it wasn't that bad, we had a game of trying to nudge each other to step onto the muddy water. But then the shoes, those canvas shoes, they would kick with some kind of liquid chalk. So we would get some, I think we would have got a scolding from our parents when they go back with muddy shoes."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): nostalgia (0.79), embarrassment (0.61), mild hurt (0.52), playfulness (0.68), resignation (0.44)
properties.emotion_all: Nostalgia, Embarrassment, Mild Hurt, Playfulness, Resignation

properties.Text: "Girls' school? Girls' school, I didn't really know about the co-ed school, but girls' school I think we were more like little sisters, you know, a band of sisters. But there was also a hidden elitism, a very scary one when we get older. When we were younger, everybody was very cheerful and running and playing together. But as we go older and we realized that we were split into classes, then that became very apparent."

"So that's where I felt a bit sad when the girls would use their intellect. And this school was quite elitist actually. Yeah. Quite pressurizing if you don't meet the grade and you get bumped off. If you're always in the B class and then you get bumped into the C and then go down. But that kind of put a little bit of damper on the very nice environment that we have. That was the biggest damper for me. At least. The competition. Yeah, the competition. So I didn't realize that even if we were at, I was middle, I think C, the C class. And I was made to feel a bit lousy in here because of all the smart girls around."

"I think, but on a national level when I finally went away to Virginia College and even to our kids, even to university school, I realized, hey, I'm not so behind after all. So that was the realization that led me back to me. Even though the girls were on a class that's far behind, maybe the environment itself, this place was kind of rich."

"Even if it was a shop house, when we step outside, we had the sense that this area is for people with higher social status. Yeah. So yeah, that feeling is I think part of the school. We were always told about the heritage that it was like for Monyans and Babas and the rich merchants who want a school to train their little girls. And so even if you compare the shop houses or the Peranakan houses here from Chinatown, you can see the difference in the treatment, in the motifs, even the social status of the people staying here. So that has come through through the years of our history also. That is a bit upmarket than Chinatown. So that feeling is still apparent here."

"Even now, coming in here, I feel this space is quite luxurious for a single person to stay at that time. It would be nice to see what had been in here. If I had come in as a little girl, I would have thought that this would have been the humongous mansion. Now, I think it's big. Imagine that at that age, I think it would have been enormous for me. So yeah, it's just amazing to come in here. I'm not sure what to say now. I'm just soaking in the experience."

"In the present? I actually have not been back for two decades or more at least. Wow. Yeah, except for the time when I came here to trespass through the science block. We came through here and also we went all the way. There was a night watchman. So we saw his torch. Oh my goodness. So we tried to avoid. We went in the opposite direction and went up to the secondary school block. The Lee Kong Chien block. All the way to the rooftop and we sat there under the stars."

"I like the scale of the school. My daughter was in SCGS Dun & Road for about six years. So I could send her to school and could see the interiors. I was also a parent teacher volunteer at that time. But this school is like, comparing here, the scale here is very intimate. The other side is big. I like the space there. The little kids these days have a lot more space to play with. Because of the intimacy of the scale here, every corner is used. You see girls sitting in the corner here playing that four stones. Everything is colonised by the girls."

"Freedom and safety? I guess comfort? I think a little while ago, I remember that there was this gate, kind of like a night watchman. We don't really interact with him, but he would come and close and lock all the gates. So even when we tried to push the gates here, last time the gates here, we can see a big lock there, we cannot go out. While we were in school. So only that little gate out there was... People could walk in now. But we didn't dare to really... For us, I'm not sure. Maybe we've been programmed not to get out beyond the boundaries during school time. So there was a sense of... We felt safe in the school. This was intimate and we can see each other."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): safety (0.78), comfort (0.72), mild fear (0.41), discipline (0.64), belonging (0.69)
properties.emotion_all: Safety, Comfort, Mild Fear, Discipline, Belonging

properties.Text: "For girls that study in CHIJ, they will feel that loss. So I've not been to CHIJ as a school before. I've gone in after it has been transformed. And I thought the spaces in there were quite well done. Some of it is still very well connected. You go inside, you go to the eateries and there are pockets of space that you turn around the corner and you see something else. So I think for anybody else other than an SCGS girl, the transformation would be different. So if we just be very selfish about it and just give us a very personal SCGS feel, we will lose that familiarity. But if we are open to that change, we can also view it, take it more on an objective level like CHIJ."

"The outside... I think the... I enjoy the corridors out there. The five foot way. So... Walking down... I think when I graduated to taking the public transport, walking down through the five foot way is also quite an experience. It's something that I find that it sets a different feel from after you exit Emerald Hill itself onto the Orchard Road. That difference is felt even as a child. You see, sometimes when you walk past the five foot way, you see residents inside. I was like, wow. Then you can peek at the... And then after that, you step onto the main street. And then there's bustling traffic there."

"So I was always curious to see who these Centre Point kids are. Because we've been taught to be good girls, you know. Well behaved. So I always wanted to see what's the difference. So I don't really know now. I still don't."

"But every little girl, I think most of the cohort went through ballet. But I think that training was important, just like running, although you may not like it. But that grace, the way you act, the poise, you know. I think the uniform also forces us to stand properly, sit properly. So yes, it helped now, the habits of all these. But of course when you are in your PE shorts, hell breaks loose."

"Okay, I will. I'm just thinking how to... I'm just soaking it all in because I've actually seen many images of this courtyard. And I assume it's where they assemble every morning. For morning assembly. Yeah, let's... I'm going to go up here. I'm just shocked at how small it is because the current one is so much bigger."

"Oh wow. The photos must be quite spatially clear. Yeah. I noticed how low the ceiling is. Not sure why. No. Okay. This must have been some form of like a bookshop? Tuckshop thing. Yeah. First impressions of this thing? Yeah, I'm wondering if this was for like the primary school... Oh, okay. This is actually shared. Yeah. Was this also for all 10 cohorts? So like P1 to P4? All 10. I think this should be secondary 4 and then that should be the primary block. Yeah. It's primary block and that's the secondary... I think this and that one would be higher. Okay. Yeah. Yeah, primary and secondary. Morning and afternoon session."

"It's very similar to the Dun & Road campus where you enter from the same entrance and then you have one same school canteen. And then the primary and secondary have very clearly demarcated locations. Does it feel similar for you? Do you feel like it's something... Okay, the current one, the existing one has two courtyards. So there's a main courtyard when you enter the school. So very similar to what I experienced here when we came in. But then what happened is that there is a second courtyard behind. Right. Yeah, so it's like double. And then of course, morning assembly was separate. Yeah. So secondary school had their own morning assembly, primary school had their own morning assembly. But I imagine this would have been all 10, unless it was different sessions, morning and afternoon. When we saw some of the archive photos, they were packed. Yeah, it's too small."

"This is tiny. This is a classroom. This is nice. This is really big. First impressions. I was going to say it smells a bit like that typical classroom dusty, with a slightly sweaty smell. But I imagine it's just dust from an extended period of non-usage here. Right. I guess, of course, if this space were to be refurbished, there's a lot of work that needs to be done."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): nostalgia (0.74), sensory recall (0.82), mild discomfort (0.44), practicality (0.39)
properties.emotion_all: Nostalgia, Sensory Recall, Mild Discomfort, Practicality

properties.Text: "I never knew it was so near the city. Yeah. Because when you see it, you just see it as an old building. You don't register it as the old SCGS. Oh, they do have the Lee Kong Chin block. I think we have a similar block in the current campus as well. It's the same name. Oh, I see. Yeah. So, I think they brought this name of the block over to the current campus."

"This was the old principal's living quarters, I've heard. No way. Wait, can we enter this? Yeah. So, the principal stayed in school. That's really interesting, right? The students never got to visit this. Yeah. I imagine this would have been completely blocked off. This is a shop house as well. Yes, I notice a lot more... a lot more adornments and embellishments compared to the... Oh, wow. This is actually very similar to the colonial shop houses. Colonial bungalows around Malcolm Road and near Botanics. I wonder what's the historical significance of that."

"I guess what I'm wondering is why did the principal have... Why did she choose to reside within the school and not maybe in an external facility nearby? It feels quite... I'm not sure how to feel about that."

"Because, I mean, of course, when you're in school and you're a student, you will hear all these stories of sightings at night, right? So, I never saw anything, but I'm just wondering if, let's say, it's at night and then it's dark out, would it have been a bit unsafe or would there have been sightings? It's just more like a, I guess, a safety point of view."

"No, I don't. I think that it being painted this brighter shade of blue, it does give it quite a light quality about it. Very different colours, actually. I'm noticing that the school uniform is this sky blue, right? And it's very aligned with the blues of this school, which honestly makes more sense because the current campus is like a peach. It's like a pink colour. It's pink and green. They use a lot of the green like Peranakan tiles. It's not blue at all. I wonder if it's a conscious design choice to remove all of the blue, because the only thing blue in the current one is just our uniforms. It was never the façade. Hmm. That's interesting. So this is refreshing to see."

"I think in general, I would have expected to... Okay, I would say that the primary campus... is a lot more inviting than the second. This block here looks a lot more utilitarian. Right. Yeah. But in terms of if there is a natural flow of movement from when you enter to here, I would say that there's not much of a guiding... You can just kind of walk everywhere."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): preference (0.72), mild disappointment (0.63), spatial awareness (0.71), evaluation (0.68)
properties.emotion_all: Preference, Mild Disappointment, Spatial Awareness, Evaluation

properties.Text: "Yeah, see, this is what I mean by the low windows, right? This is a lot more gloomy, a lot darker energy than the primary school campus. I much prefer the primary school with classrooms. This is very depressing. Yeah. It's very depressing because you don't have much natural light coming in. Yeah. So difference in lighting and airflow. I think that's very important."

"It kind of feels like the design of the school happened in phases. It felt like it wasn't designed as a whole. And one whole project, it kind of felt... I wouldn't use the extent of patchwork, but it kind of felt as though things were built. You can kind of tell which was built before the other. Which is why I'm guessing they eventually moved to a bigger campus, because this just couldn't support the..."

"I think that it is refreshing to see the original campus and then how they transformed everything into the new campus. There is close to no similarity. Yeah, so I guess I just wonder why they decided not to retain anything. I guess the central courtyard and separating both the schools, that's a similarity, but... But yeah, I think it's definitely good that they reenergised the new campus because it's definitely better for work and for education, for learning, for play. Yeah, playgrounds as well, I didn't notice that there were no playgrounds. No, just open courtyard. Open courtyard, yeah. I guess it alluded to a simpler time in the past."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): nostalgia (0.81), appreciation (0.64), curiosity (0.59), reflective longing (0.53), acceptance (0.47)
properties.emotion_all: Nostalgia, Appreciation, Curiosity, Reflective Longing, Acceptance

properties.Text: "I would say that the neighbourhood here is a lot more pretty than the current one. Because the current one, the moment you open it, it's a huge... It's like a highway, it's a main road. There's nothing around, but here it's nice. It's almost as though you can continue to play once you exit the school. Yeah, very central. And these chump houses, they were definitely here already, right? Because they were built in the early 1900s. We heard they were different colours back then. A lot more colourful. A lot less colourful, I think. Yeah. I think this is definitely in a more affluent neighbourhood than the current one. Yeah. So, yeah, just contrasting the two."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): aesthetic appreciation (0.79), comparison (0.74), nostalgia (0.68), mild critique (0.51)
properties.emotion_all: Aesthetic Appreciation, Comparison, Nostalgia, Mild Critique

properties.Text: "Okay, so welcome to 37 Emerald Hill. Give us a tour of the space and how you feel in all of these spaces. Maybe what you think they're used for. And does your body feel comfortable here? Do you enjoy the space? Do you want something different for it in the future? Okay. I kind of want to explore that area. Yeah. Can you imagine this as a nice girl's school? Yeah, I mean, like, obviously the context is a little different, right? When it was a girl's school, but... I think so far... it's larger than what I expected, maybe? Because you're talking about how it's like the first secular girl's school, right? Yeah, I was thinking like maybe at the time... they would have prioritized Christian schools and like... at that point in Singapore's history, right? So you're surprised that they had so much land for it. Yeah. I see. And obviously Orchard wasn't necessarily Prime District at that time, but like it's still quite large for the time."

"I think this is the principal's house that they then converted into the art room. If you want to go inside. Is it? Yep. Oh. Oh, so the principal lived on site? Yes. The principal lived on site and so had a pretty close relationship with the teachers and students. Oh. Was your school similar when you were growing up? No, I mean... we had like... Oh. If you want to. Yeah, I do. You had? Um... No, like in school, like I didn't go to a boarding school or anything, I just went to like a mid-sized international school. And so I think also the architecture is like very dramatically different from what I'm used to. Can I go in? What do you notice about the architecture? I mean, well, first of all, it's beautiful. But it's also like it's very home-like. It's not... it doesn't feel stale. I mean, it feels like an environment that I think I would personally be very comfortable in growing up."

"I like that there's a lot of light coming in. Mm. I think it just... it makes it feel more homey, right? Mm. So this is the house and then they made it into the art. Mm-hmm. When the principal moved off campus, then she would still park her car right outside of this house."

"Actually, when I was like looking at the green court, right, and then this court, because I was interested previously on like the use of play and like how young girls play in school environments and... But then I was reminded that this was a girls' school, and so the dynamics are definitely a lot more different because in like co-ed schools, it's... If I remember correctly, like in places like this, these type of areas are not highly recommended because boys tend to territorialize the space and therefore girls start to like... What's the right word? Like... They... They start to condition themselves to like stand on the periphery of things rather than invite themselves into the space because boys can be so like, you know, at that age. Yes, yes. So in your childhood then, would you say you wouldn't be so comfortable to be right in the middle of a play area or sports area? 100%, yeah."
