"I think girls can actually be more inviting to one another, especially at that age and like... I think without the presence of young boys, girls tend to feel more comfortable in their own like desires. I see. And so the feeling of judgment may still be there, but it's a different type of judgment that's not like derived from gender or power."

"But I can imagine that without the experience of like having that one female friend, boys can be so terrifying. And like the social scene in like the third grade is terrifying if you don't have that best friend to lean on. Absolutely. And like at any age, right? Especially like when you're a child, it's so much scarier because I mean like you don't know... Yeah. Like you don't know how to respond, right?"

"This room is terrifying. These were laboratories. Oh, it's terrifying. Why? I mean there's no windows and there's no light. So you wouldn't want to have class here. Probably not. It wouldn't be my favorite classroom though."

"Okay, right now it feels a little bit scary, but I think that's because there's no one in here. Hmm. If you could imagine a normal day at school here, how would your body feel? Do you think you'd be comfortable? Hmm. I don't know. Do you like the design? Yeah, I mean I can imagine that with people here, I would feel like you feel close to your friends. Hmm. Why is that? Because of the windows, right? Hmm. So you can always see people, you know. Yeah. Okay, I don't, I'm scared to go there. Oh, you're scared. It's just dark."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): fear (0.72), uncertainty (0.69), longing (0.58), desire for belonging (0.61)
properties.emotion_all: Fear, Uncertainty, Longing, Desire For Belonging

properties.Text: "To me, because in my school, we grew up with these ginormous windows everywhere. Light was a really big thing in the architecture of my school. I see. And so I associate light with safety. I see. And it's time to go to school. So the daytime as well as education. Which is why passing, when you do extracurriculars and you pass 536 and it gets dark, then it starts to feel like, okay, I'm not supposed to be here. I see. Yeah, so light was really like, maybe this is why this is not like, it doesn't feel safe to me."

"These children's classrooms are like the best classrooms I've seen in my life. Like they are so conducive to like what children want and like having fun. And like my school is very learning by play methodology. And so the entire like preschool and junior, like kindergarten classroom is like built around that concept of allowing a child to be creative within boundaries."

"I think it takes away from like the like historic presence of it, right? So the fact that it's low rise. Yeah, the low rise. And then... It's a really special feeling about us being nested. Yes, for sure."

"I felt really out of place in Orchard despite the fact that our hotels were always at Orchard. We stayed here all the time. I would meet my friends here. For some reason, as a young 11, 12, I think I was probably 11, I started coming back when I was 11, I felt really out of place. Why is that? It just didn't feel like a place a child should be in. And it felt like I was performing and trying to be an adult when I was there. As a young girl. Yeah, I obviously didn't have the financial capital to be buying everything I wanted to buy, but I would still go in the stores and I would browse and I would hang out with my friends and it felt like we weren't really, we didn't really gel together with what Orchard represented it feels like."

"Now, since coming back to Singapore for college, I think I definitely feel a lot more comfortable in Orchard. And I think a big part of that is one, just being a little bit older and being a little bit less, being more comfortable in my skin, I think. And just like now I'm in university, like I've grown up and I feel like I can enjoy the space and also familiarity."

"It feels upsetting to see them like be so public. I don't know what the word is like. People talk about it all the time as if it's like a public nuisance despite the fact that we're all using the space. We're just using the space a little bit differently."

"Wow, I didn't even know this place existed. Do you like the space? Yeah, I love it. Like, I could not look at this. Like, I could not imagine living here. You would want to? Yeah, I would want to. It feels very private. It feels private. It feels disconnected from Orchard. Like, I don't feel like I'm in Orchard right now. Ah. Is it because there's a lack of commercialization? Yeah. I see. And, like, the style of the house is the same. What do the style of the house mean for you? I'm trying to find a word for it. It's so vibrant. Vibrant. Vibrant. Like, color is not something we see in modern architecture, I feel."

"I think everybody would desire to have this place conserved. I mean, at least that would be my assumption. That you wouldn't want to just throw all this history away. Right. But... I think maybe they need to see how important it is to Singaporeans. Mm. And... I'm not sure how you get that word out there."

"Yeah, I mean, like, time is more precious now that you're working, and so I think there would need to be a reason for me to come down here. Okay. Like a utility or a service you could get here? Like a service or even like a... Like if there was a cafe down there, I would be more than happy to walk down. It's just that as of right now, I literally don't hear anything about Emerald Hill."

"No, I've never been here before. I didn't even know that this was here, or among Emerald Hill. My only impression of Emerald Hill is the really commercial streets. No, not often. Only to grab a drink at the bars nearby. That's what I know it for, but I don't know it for any other. Yeah, 100%. I guess the architecture, like the Peranakan influence, I think I think of, but not anything other than that."

"It feels really dark. Like... Dark. Yeah, considering how hot it is out now. Well, obviously there's no light going on right now, and it's really low too. The girls' school, right? So like younger kids. Yes, yeah. But it's really low. I don't know. I feel like the space is smaller than it has to be because the ceilings are lower. So you would have imagined being more comfortable if the ceiling was higher. A little, yeah. I would feel more comfortable."

"Just I feel like the open air... Coming from the entrance, it makes sense that the canteen is first. Food, actually, I don't know. Was your school similar? No, actually. I guess not. But the same kind of like alfresco canteen situation was the same, but not close to the entrance. I think this is what I have an impression of Singaporean schools though. Like this type of open air. So you expect Singaporean schools to have that. Did you study abroad? No, I didn't study. Yes, I studied abroad, but I didn't study here. I grew up in the Philippines and then moved to Singapore for uni."
"Oh my gosh, they're so small. That's so crazy. So it feels very much a space intended for young girls. Yes, it does. And unusable to older girls. Yeah, how it is now."

"It feels good. It's like better ventilated than I thought it would be, I guess. But I imagine that it would get like hotter around there because it's really hot day out now. So I'm like cognizant of how the air feels on me, I guess. Yeah, yeah. I see. It's actually. Do you like this walk? Yeah, I like this walk. If this were to be reimagined, I would like this to stay open, you know. An open field? Yeah, an open field or an open space."

"But I feel like I don't frequent Orchard because there's no, everything feels so claustrophobic. Like having another space if they had to reimagine the space, have an open field where there's no particular commercial agenda. I don't know, like you know how in One Holland Village, there are kind of, there are seats and there are just open areas and trees. Right. That's totally missing in Orchard. I know it's not meant to be that way, but it would be nice to have that break. So you'd want like a natural public space in Orchard. Yeah, I would. I see. And I feel like that's just generally missing in Orchard."

"Do you like this hallway? I guess so. It feels cooler. I'm super cognizant of temperature now because it's so hot. Because the brick feels cooler, the concrete feels cooler. I'm welcoming the breeze that feels cooler. So breeze is very important in this outdoor space. I would say so."

"What do you think of this space? I don't like it. No. It makes me feel trapped. And this is a laboratory, you said, right? I get that, but nary a window. Nary a window or ventilation or anything. I would feel isolated and I can't imagine being P1 or P2 or whatever and having to stay here for multiple hours. No, I wouldn't feel free. It feels very sanitary. Not sanitary, that's not the right word, but I don't know. It doesn't smell like it. It feels not so human. No, not human at all."
"Yeah, this looks quite similar to the school, like my school bathrooms. Just the colored stalls and all that stuff. They have braids. It's cute."
"It does feel dated. They're so consistent with the blue."

"I don't know, I think these feel like a room. I don't know, I think these feel, it feels nostalgic to me. I see why they wanted to keep this space. Not that my school looked very similar, but just walking through the hallways and seeing all the doors lined up one by one, I can almost imagine what those girls would have been like inside the various classrooms. I don't know, maybe just sitting down or hearing the noise of classrooms, chatter, a bell ringing, something like that. It invokes feelings of nostalgia, I feel like."

"I think it's just meaningful in general to have spaces that are conducive for nostalgia at any stage of your life, and public spaces that are accessible to invoke those feelings. I mean especially as a foreigner that doesn't know what necessarily a secondary school... I have no lived experience being in a primary school or a secondary school, but having this be accessible to me or that I know about it now is valuable. It adds color to what I would imagine the lived experience of a Singaporean girl could be like, to some degree, and I think that's important for people to have."

"I guess? Not really, it kind of looks like a hospital. I don't know. I guess my impression of just the wired, the block-ish windows. I don't know, it just seems more like a hospital to me. I don't know why."

"I think it's nice. It feels grandiose and strategic to be in the middle, and then you can see if it's like you mentioned where the kids can line up and be picture ready and getting an overhead view of the school. There's something traditional about it. Yeah, it feels very traditional."

"Generic. I caution saying generic because I feel like when I hear the word charming, I think of details and I think of quirks. And right now, because it's been stripped of a lot of the details and quirks, I think I can't fully say charming."

"Yes, for sure. And even though I can tell it's been lived in because it's being teared down, it's older, it's harder for me to imagine life for girls running around without like signs of their breasts being there. I don't know, pencils or like chairs or the braids. Yeah, that was that was really charming. I was like, oh, that's a cute detail that they added in. The choice that they made. Yeah, it was a choice that they made for the girls for no particular reason."
"Yeah, it's super endearing. I find it funny and endearing and they should keep this. They should keep this. Yes."

"I think it feels older, like everything from like the higher ceilings and bigger doors. I mean, you mentioned to me too, it was for older kids, but I would have been able to guess too, I think. That, yeah, that it was meant for older kids. Yeah, it does feel academic to me. Less like a hospital. It feels academic because it just matches the expectations of what I think a school would be structured like. I would like to go to school here. I guess so. There's not much reason for me to say no, but."

"Yeah, I definitely feel quite boxed in by the buildings, the residential buildings. The difference is quite stark now that I'm looking at it now. Like with the architecture, the grays, the colors. The difference is stark when you're looking up from here."

"I think it would be nice if it stayed low rise. But I also am saying that from someone who has this perspective of Singapore always wants to build up. Everything has to be technologic, like everything has to be spatially efficient. We should have some low rise stuff. So I'm coming from that perspective too. So I would like it to be low rise."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): reflection (0.71), desire for balance (0.74), mild critique (0.52)
properties.emotion_all: Reflection, Desire For Balance, Mild Critique

properties.Text: "I like the the design. I like the architecture. It's cute. But I have no other, like sentimentality or any other feelings of fondness towards it aside from like visually, I guess."

"It feels aspirational. I don't know if that's the right word. Like, awesome. I don't know, I think in general, I find shop houses with this style of architecture to be very charming and very pretty but also inaccessible to me. Like renting for example in a shop house residentially would be really expensive. So I don't know, it feels like aspirational to me for some reason."

"I think conserving the school could have a very grounding relationship to the street. Where I see aspiration and development and commercial Singapore. If you could very easily turn to your right and then there's a piece of what the street would have looked like before it turned. Maybe preserving this piece of history would be something really different from this commercialized area. For sure. And I guess that would also make the space more diverse."

"Like, I don't know if Orchard has any parks. What if this just turned into a park? I think there's something like sweet or sentimental to me if it was a school and it kept an element of play. It kept an element of openness in relation to the school. And like, childhood. I would like it to be like in Tanjung Pagar where you have those buildings but then you have in the middle the park that you walk through. I appreciate that space still. As a break from the city to sit down. Orchard to me feels like a huge commercial runway. There's no space to sit in corners that you can kind of escape to or just sit down in or like see as a resting spot or a third space or a play space that isn't something commercial or to be paid for. So I think it would be a good idea."

"I don't really enjoy Orchard. It's busy. For me I find it difficult to navigate. I don't like how everything's like to navigate everything underground. It feels like a big runway. No, I don't love Orchard to be honest."

"I think it should be preserved and I think it's as active as it should be in that it holds space for that memory and holds space for that part of history. I think it's valuable for me as a foreigner that never went to school in Singapore to know this and to see this. I don't think it should change aesthetics. It should stay the way it is. It's valuable for that it stays that way."

"Shading would be really important. This whole roof situation is making it conducive for us to walk around. If it was fully bright, I wouldn't be able to, we wouldn't be able to be so freely walking around and not be uncomfortable. Air conditioning could be a welcome release though. But I don't know if it's necessarily conducive to that open space park-like envisionment that I have. It's just like utilitarian."

"Oh wow, so when was the last time you were in the school? Like four decades, four and a half decades ago. Wow. Did you do all of your schooling here? Yes, from primary to secondary. Oh wow. Which year did you start? 1971 to 1980, and I still have the school magazines. And I have my primary one school uniform too, which I forgot to bring to show you. That would be amazing. Bring it to the drawing workshop. Oh, okay. Yeah, so how does it feel entering the campus after four decades? Yeah, it's like very surreal. And of course, my fondest memories would be the primary school block."

"All right, this block was the last block to be built. We were running out of space, and we needed space for the school hall for a bigger canteen. And also, I think they built this. And during that time, we had to raise funds. Yes, to build this. Yes, the children."

"Well, I mean, previously, this was not barricaded. And so, we didn't have that, you know, we didn't have like mobile games and things like that. So, our games were like created by us. So, we used to run up and down, to see who could run up and down how many times without falling. Yeah, and one time I rolled down. Oh gosh. Because we were so excited. So, that was the slope, and that's the secondary one class classroom. So, before, there was no fence, and this grass was all... Yeah, it's all grass. Yes, so no fence. And so, we would run up and down, and it was really fun, just entertaining each other. And then, of course, sometimes we make too much noise, the teacher will come out too. And the one who came out to scold us, eventually became our school principal."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): nostalgia (0.91), joy (0.83), amusement (0.52), excitement (0.64), attachment / belonging (0.67)

properties.Text: "This was my primary one, classroom. Oh, when I went to primary one, I couldn't really speak English very well because I lived with my grandparents and they spoke dialect. So, half the time, I couldn't really understand what the teacher was saying. But slowly, I picked up. Because most of the students here could speak English very well. So, they slowly picked up. And I also learned from my classmates. Sometimes, I read their essays. When they get very high marks, I would read their essays to understand how they wrote, what they wrote, you know. And from through them, I also started borrowing books. During my hour time, it was like those Nancy Drew stories."

"And of course, when the teacher is angry with us, she will fling our exercise book from the classroom out over here. And if the teacher is not happy, like, how can you make this mistake? And she would just fling the book and throw it out to the hall. And we'd run out to take our books. Wow. Yeah, now they would not be able to do such a thing. The parents would have complained."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): fear or worry (0.46), amusement (0.51), nostalgia (0.72), resignation (0.33)
properties.emotion_all: Fear Or Worry, Amusement, Nostalgia, Resignation

properties.Text: "Oh, yes. On this side? I love it. What was the view before? Oh, definitely not high rise. Probably just very short buildings and like maybe shop houses. I can't really remember exactly, but definitely not so high."

"But I just had nightmares about these two flights of stairs. Because it's quite, you know, it's quite dark. In fact, I don't know if you can see it, it's always very dark and it's not like lit. So over here was a picture of the founder of the school, Mr. Lin Boon King. And every time I look at the picture, I'm so glad I'm scared. Because he looks so stunned and with his beard and all."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): fear or worry (0.89), anxiety (0.61), nostalgia (0.58), amusement (0.29)

properties.Text: "And this back door is so useful to us because we would run down to the court to choke, we call it choke the court, to book the court. So once, so we just waiting for the bell to ring and the minute the bell rang, we will dash out from this end because it's the fastest way to get to the court. And then we will choke the place to, yes. Yeah, so then here you would play four square and different games. Like badminton. And this game called, we call it saloon, but some people call it... Or rounders. Yeah, it's like we use a tennis ball and then we hit."

"So every day after school, you'd line up for the school bus? After school, we just walked straight to the school buses. For those of us who take school bus. But then there are some whose parents come to fetch them, and some who, if they take the public transport, they will walk down Emerald Hill. Previously, it was not 313 Somerset. It was called Specialist Centre. And there was a car park there. They call it Carpage. At night, in the day, it's a car park. At night, it becomes a hawker centre. Open air."

"And while waiting for the school bus to drive off to send us back, we would spend our time picking rubber seats. There were a lot of rubber trees here. So, there were a lot of rubber seats. And then we would compete as to who can pick up the most. Wow. So, we ended up with bags and bags at home. And they didn't know what to do with it. And rubber seats, you know, you can rub it on the floor. And dry. It's very hot. Yes. It would burn you."

"And finally, one day, they told us that there will be a male belief teacher. And everybody got very excited. Because we had never had a male teacher. And one of the questions they asked was, which toilet is he going to?"
"Oh, yes. Yes. And I liked it. You liked it? Yes. Yeah, I think there's something about the friendships you can have, right? And all girls' school."

"My favourite class? I think I enjoyed my primary school the most. And I love the, my school decided, I mean, most primary schools were just, English, Chinese, math and science. But my school decided to do subjects like literature, history. And national language and things like that. So, I really enjoyed their literature lessons. And the teachers were very, in fact, even in my secondary school, they were very, very good. They started from the book, they made the whole thing like, as if all the characters were alive in our classroom. I really love it. They were really so good."

"And Indian ink, when you spill it, the stain is quite bad. So I was very excited. I thought I finished. I said, ah, I finished. And then, boom. Oh, no. Everything all spilled. I was all like... So they had to bring me to the teacher's room to get some. Sometimes you have old girls who donate their uniform, their old uniforms to the school. So you borrow. So I just had to borrow one of them to go home."

"She was very open because she was a lot younger. And she was very open to anything. So during that time, we didn't even have a school t-shirt. We only had a plain white t-shirt. And then to differentiate between the houses, we had red, blue, yellow, green. All we had was just like a strap. So she was very open to having these. And also very open. We didn't even have our school file with our logo and things like that. Nothing at all. So she did a lot of these things. She started to revamp everything. So that's why many people love her. She was very, very open. And suddenly the school changed."
"Well, we got used to it. We'll just go back very sweaty and dirty."

"I think it's quite rare. I feel that these fences should be taken out. But I think, I believe that maybe the Australian school that was using it, probably they had to fence it up so that when they had balls and things like that, the thing wouldn't fly out. But it loses the... Charm. Yes, the charm. Correct."
"So when I think about the school, it's always this image. Yes."
"Not really. Okay, we'll end the video then. Yeah, thank you. Okay. Thank you."

"Great. Okay, so we're walking to a... That's fun, we're having the play in the primary school. So people will swing around the area. Oh, on the bars? Yeah, like you just... Please don't video me doing this, like this. And they play catching, so you just change corners and everyone goes to a different bar. So the one who's left out is the one who's stuck with the thing. We're all dumped out of school bags here when we're waiting in a line. If we were in an afternoon session, we're waiting for the morning session to come out, so we're all dumped out of school bags here. And then just wait for the session to come out so that, you know, and just hang around here, so it's a playing area."

"No, the main entrance is open. I mean, I started here in primary one, morning session, so my dad would drop me off at the end of the road. And I think he would walk me in, I don't know. And I can't remember. And then my primary classroom was there."

"This area looks the same, except the pillars used to be yellow. Yellow? Yeah. So, but the pillars are exactly the same, they're not that gross. But they're exactly the same here. This is the same. This part's all the same. This drain was uncovered. I fell in a couple of times. Fell in? You don't really fall in, but you know, you run and then you trip and then you... Was it wet when you fell in? No, you just scraped your knee here at this level. Oh, gosh. Yeah. So, yeah, this area... And people would trip over this when we are running around and playing. So you trip and fall over here. There are many levels in this entrance. Yes. So this is the playing area from a primary school."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): nostalgia (0.86), amusement (0.52), mild pain memory (0.49), attachment / belonging (0.45)
properties.emotion_all: Nostalgia, Amusement, Mild Pain Memory, Belonging

properties.Text: "Okay, some really weird memories. This is the mee pot stall. I'm sure everyone talks about it. It's the famous mee pot stall. I won't really talk about it, but I remember the guy, the helper, was sitting here like Pukie one time. Oh, gosh. There's also a toilet back there. There was a... I think the case of food poisoning at one point. Oh, so everyone was rushing there. Minor, minor... No, that's the toilet for the staff to use. For the staff. So inside here is the canteen. Here... It looks... everything looks smaller now. Smaller? So that's the mee pot stall. It will cover this part. Inside, I can't remember. There's a chicken rice stall here. You can buy chicken rice for 30 cents then. Wow, yeah. Then some mish-mash things that I probably don't eat. There's a stall selling tidbits right at the side. So they'll sell hot dogs like maybe 20 cents."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): nostalgia (0.91), amusement (0.55), disgust (0.46), surprise (0.38), attachment / belonging (0.44)

properties.Text: "Okay, so I didn't know how to order mee pot in Chinese because my Chinese is very bad. So I ate chicken rice for a very long time. And that's okay. All that hot dog because it just points. So my friend had to teach me how to order mee pot in... in dialect and Chinese. So that's about primary 3. Oh wow. So the auntie will cook her mee pot there. And the uncle, his mee pot is nicer. He'll cook on this side. So this queue is always longer than that one. But everyone will mention the famous mee pot. And the guy goes tok tok tok outside. I think this is where the drinks were. Yeah, so this was actually the drink stall. Drinks. Yeah, so the drinks... The bottle drinks are here. And then a lot of cups. You got 10 cents for rose syrup and then 20 cents for soft drink."

"Yeah, so for $1 you can get... That's $1 you can get your lunch. 80 cents. Yeah. So this is... Oh, this is the same. This is the same. It's so weird because it's been so long and they have kept all this. So the tap... This came later. This wasn't here when I was here in 1981. I think they built it later. Yeah, so it's quite strange that they kept this."

"So you get long tables. So it's all placed this way. Then you get benches along the side. So we're normally... It's normally quite full during recess. So everyone just goes there and tries to get a table. Because everyone has recess at the same time. Ah, okay. In rushing the choke. Yeah, rushing. So primary school, all the primary school kids are out at the same time. Then you also have secondary school. I think it's slightly staggered. But you still have all the primary school kids out at the same time."

"Recess time, yes. We try and show up to see. This is the bookstore. Now it's sealed up. This is the second bookstore. The first bookstore is somewhere else. So there's a sliding grate thing here. And then there's the... I can't remember his name, but he sold all our textbooks. Our workbooks, stationery and stuff. I don't know what that is. That's new. How often would you come to this bookstore? If you got extra pocket money, you go and buy nonsense like erasers. So he got in trouble once because he sold extra stuff like fancy erasers. And then we all got away some money on that. And then you buy rubber bands for your skipping rope. So I was skipping ropes on me out of Intertwine rubber bands for zero dollars. Or a coin. One dollar."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): nostalgia (0.90), amusement (0.66), attachment / belonging (0.48), mild loss (0.31)

properties.Text: "Okay, this is interesting. I've never been inside. I've always been so curious. So what was this place? They said it was the house. Is this like... Can go in? Yes. Wow. It's my first time. It was... I don't know actually. They said it used to belong to one of the former principals or something, but it's always locked up. So this is my first time going in. So it's very exciting."
"I'm neutral. Neutral? It doesn't have an emotional memory for me. So like 10 years, so I guess a lot of things are tied to emotions. Of course. So that..."
"I like this place a lot, because I spent 10 years here, and I'm very attached to SCGS."
"I don't think I would. Oh you wouldn't? No. Why not? Because it's my... Even now it doesn't look quite the same so it doesn't quite have the same feel."

"Okay, we're recording now. When was the last time you were here? 1980, when I left. And how long were you schooling here? So, I started in Primary 4, so... How far is it? Seven years. So, this was the tuck shop. The tuck shop? Yes. Oh, what's that? Huh? Canteen. Canteen. This whole part. Do you remember the orientation? Yeah, vaguely. The most famous stall in SCGS was the mee pok stall. Oh! It's very famous. All these office workers used to try and sneak in from there. So, we used to have our prefects guarding there to shoo them away. The office workers! Because they could come in and have the mee pok. It was so famous and also it was cheap, right? I see. But yeah, it was very famous. So, the mee pok stall, I think, was somewhere around here. And the drink stall was over there. And there was a kachamputti man next to the drink stall. And do you know what is kachamputti? Yeah."

"Was it your daily meal? My mother was rather... She never gave me money to buy tuck shop food because she said it was unhealthy. So, I used to sell the food that she gave me so that I could have some money to buy tuck shop... Buy the mee pok. So, what food would you sell? Oh, my mother had gourmet food that I would bring to school like nasi lemak, lamb cutlets with mushroom and... So, the girls bought from you? Yeah, but I wanted the junk."

"So, this was our sports field renewal and primary school. And also, our sports day was here, our national day celebrations were all here. But there was real grass at that time. This is AstroTurf, I think, now, sadly. And there was no caging before? No, this is cage. It looks like a zoo. Maybe because they were playing ball games, so they need to... But yeah."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): nostalgia (0.84), disappointment (0.63), loss / grief (0.55), attachment / belonging (0.49)

properties.Text: "So, when I came here, I was in primary four. So, it was a bit of a culture shock for me because I came from Tanglin Trust School. Oh. And then I came here. And my first classroom was ground floor, the back, primary four. We can go in if you'd like. Yeah, oh, okay. I'll show you. It was nice because it was the only classroom that had both a front and a back entrance. So, we had quick access to the playground. So, we could run out and choke the courts to play our games. Oh my."

"What changes are you noticing? I don't think there's a toilet there. I remember that this was where the... You know, the government had this scheme to... give girls who were not so well-nourished milk at recess time. But it wasn't very nice milk. So, they forced them to come here and they would drink the milk. Oh, that's like a milk movement. Sorry? Is it like a milk room where they saw the milk? I don't know. Sorry. I don't remember because I was not malnourished."

"So, how was the adjustment actually from Tanglin Trust? It was a huge shock, I must admit. How so? I was quite traumatised. Well, firstly, the class size was suddenly 44. And before it was? At Tanglin, it was like under 20. Oh, wow. And secondly, the teachers were very mean in my view."

"Oh, this is all blocked off now. This is all blocked off? Yeah, this is very different. So, this was my primary four classroom. See, there's a back entrance here. So, the first classroom you had here was this building? Yes, it was here, this room. And see what I mean? You could run out to the back, straight down to the basketball courts for our games. So, we could run down quickly and choke the court. Do you remember where you were sitting? Yeah, I do. I was sitting here. The desks were against the wall for this side. And I was somewhere around here. Because it was done by height, right? Oh, I see. So, the shortest were in front. And the blackboard is here. Yes, yes."

"I had an extremely mean class teacher. Oh, God, I hate it now. Sorry. Don't be. Very traumatic. And this was the bookshop right across. And you could enter the bookshop from here and from outside. Oh. Yeah. But now they've closed it all. They've changed it quite a bit."

"And then there was a staircase on the left. Now I know it's here. There are both sides. Ah, okay. But both are blocked off. Oh, I see. So, this staircase went up to the upper floors. So, I remember in primary five, we were upstairs. And our primary six classroom was top floor, left corner. And that one I also remember very well because the roof was leaking. And so, at the back, those who were sitting in the last three or four rows, we had to move our desks so that we were right next to each other. So, we were really happy because I met them and we could chat."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): nostalgia (0.86), joy (0.58), attachment / belonging (0.61), mild hardship (0.43)

properties.Text: "So, did you feel that the school was well maintained when you were attending the school? Ah, I guess so. I mean, I don't know. Never thought of it. No, I didn't really think about it. I mean, when you're 10, it's not something you really think about."

"Was it a very different school compared to Tanglin Trust in its design and energy? Yeah, less open spaces. Less open spaces. Tanglin Trust had a lot of... Because Tanglin Trust then... Well, I was in what was called Raban Park. They merged into... Three schools merged into one. So, Raban Park was actually where Raban Park still is. Somewhere near... Talok Blangah around there. Okay. There's actually a Raban Park Road. Yes, yes."
"Oh, this is the toilets here, which is another traumatic experience. Why traumatic? Because it's all so smelly. Smelly."

"Yes, as I said, that was the entrance to the bookshop. And this was not a covered walkway at that time. There was no tin covering? No, nothing. The covering was only this part into the... Is this the Song Ong Siang block, I think? At least, I think so. Yeah. So, that was the covered walkway so that... You didn't get wet if you were walking from the... Because that was where the main entrance was. And then, you walked through the covered walkway. And this is where we were in secondary. All the secondary classes were here and here. So, this was Sec 1 and Sec 2 was down there. And then, Sec 3 and Sec 4 were here."

"Do you have fond memories of any of these buildings? Or are you attached to any? I mean... Let's put it this way. I was happy with my friends. I wasn't really happy with the teaching, but... I see. That was really not... I mean, the teachers were not great. Some of them were really good and some were terrible. And I must say, some of the teachers had some of the... I mean, especially my primary four one. I think she had some major anger management issues. Oh, wow. Yeah, she used to throw our books out of the window, hit us on the head with the blackboard duster. Which was a regular occurrence. And she would do this to all the students, generally? Yeah, and to be fair, to be honest, what I found particularly unfair was... She was nicer to those kids whom she knew..."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): attachment / belonging (0.66), sadness (0.71), anger (0.84), injustice (0.79), trauma (0.88), disappointment (0.73)
properties.emotion_all: Belonging, Sadness, Anger, Injustice, Trauma, Disappointment

properties.Text: "Welcome back to 37 Emerald Hill. Can you take us on a tour of anywhere you'd like to go? Sure. This is my first time back in the location since I graduated. In the location? Like even the area? I mean inside the school. I've never been back for years."

"So this obviously is the canteen, but this entire space here used to be all banana trees. Banana trees? Yeah. So we had a lot of horror stories about Pontianak and everything. I never saw the back of this before. It was full of trees that covered the entire space. So you're not sure if it was a road before? Yeah, we couldn't see the road. It was just full of banana trees. So we had a lot of ghost stories that we shared at that time, especially when we had overnight camps."

"This store used to be the mee pok store. The famous mee pok. The famous mee pok for SCGS. So it's interesting. I mean like this brings a lot of memories because when the bell rang, we all used to run to see how fast we could run and the queue just starts here. So all the girls were all lining up here. It was the busiest. If I remember correctly, this is like it goes in it's actually a U-shape inside. So all the stores are in a U-shape area. Yeah, so these people they could just queue here. Line and line. Yeah lunch it was."

"So I remember that we would order here. This is the first interview we've done this. Yeah I see it's a U-shape. If I remember correctly. So all the stores. So it's literally a U and then yeah we could buy all our stuff around the corner. It was always I mean can you imagine the entire school we only had this space for canteen. It's like a really small space but we had all the long tables. It was a long table, benches. We had the wooden benches and long tables so all the long tables were here. And it was very specific how the primary school girls would be here, the secondary school girls would be here. We all had our own spaces."

"I was a primary school girl. So after 10 years? I was the last batch here before we moved. So we sort of did the fundraising and then sec four I finished here in June and then we moved to the new school. So I spent half, I was here until sec four June and then I graduated from the Whitney or Whitley Road. Yes correct so we did a lot of fundraising but we always felt lucky that we were able to enjoy the new school after fundraising for so many years. So we did both of that. So we did the big move. Yes we did a walk I think. Yeah if I can remember, this was many years ago. Yeah so this was really memorable."

"Like we would actually order the mee pok here and then the best part of the mee pok was us coming back to add all the different sauces. And the soup and everything, extra chilli, extra vinegar. So we all came back for two or three rounds of whatever. More condiments and soup. And then you take it back to your primary tables. Yeah we did lots of stuff. I mean I spent 10 years here so we hung around here. Yeah I just remember us running really quickly and making a lot of very loud stomping sounds. Stomping sounds because then we could hear us running like chasing each other to the front of the queue."

"This is like all our, it's a basin and yeah so everybody. So you could look at each other while you wash your hands after food. Yeah where do you see this? I forgot that this was even here. It was all the sinks. See it's exactly the same. They didn't move it maybe because the water faucet is here. And it's so charming you can tell it's for young girls. Yes so there's the lower ones for the shorter girls and the higher ones for the bigger girls. Yeah so this was quite cool."

"We yeah it was really nice because we, it's a Peranakan style over there so we had like French windows and all the grills with the balcony so it's very very pretty and we had a lot of Peranakan tiles on the floor so our batch really enjoyed moving into a new school. It was exciting. It was exciting and it was much bigger. At that time no we were very happy that we were in the new school but after that I think the school got sold to different schools, transfer or something and I always saw it from behind."

"The grandest part of this building. The double staircase. Was the staircase. Where is the staircase? It's gone oh my gosh. My staircase is gone. It's like yeah they've walled them up. So it's gone the staircase is gone. No no it's just it's just dark. I don't know if it's the original staircase but there's still two. Beautiful. I think it's stone so it's like it goes up like that. Oh wow so they've changed it. No it's not like that. So it was two big staircases. Curving up. Curving up. Wow. This is very stressful for my memory. Yes definitely not like this not like this. It was a wide staircase. Yeah so this is fake right."

"Yeah because it's like a like a I don't know like a palace. For us it's a very very very long and it's all gray. You know like those stones that are very well polished. Like granite. Yeah and over time it just becomes very used and aged and very beautiful. Then we just keep on rolling our hands up and down the big handrails."

"We practiced our national day songs here. Yeah in our time. You know we have a lot of national day songs that we can memorize forever like Stand Up for Singapore. So many. But there were a lot of songs that we actually sang and we loved and we memorized. But now I feel like the songs we don't really know what the national songs are anymore. They don't make all the primary school, all the kids learn it."

"So I think the message was that we were very excited to be here. The identity of this building is very, this is the main building of the school. This is the essence. This is how everything started. This is the central part of the school. And it has more history because the building itself. And it's unique because the other side is just a regular building. Whereas this one, you can't see it anymore, but they had big French windows and super high ceiling."

"So this is where, when we were in primary school, we all came here and we all squatted along this to learn how to brush our teeth. So we learned how to brush our teeth, and we spit. Oh my gosh. You know, everybody had a cup and a toothbrush, they teach us how to brush our teeth, we all squatted along. A whole class, class by class. Primary school, that's what they taught us."

"It was real grass. We hated it. Because it was... Dirty, yeah. Was it really sunny all the time? Yes, it was sunny. We hated it. But it was a time when we saw the whole school together every morning."

"I remember when we had every Chinese New Year on the day, I think Chinese New Year Eve, we could wear our own Chinese New Year clothes. So all the girls are so... Finally not in uniform. Yes, they love to wear out their fancy clothes. It was the highlight of our year. The balconies over there, we would be hanging there around. You can't see it anymore, but the balconies, all the girls would hang out. We'd take so many photos in our New Year clothes by the balcony. I've got memories of that."

"We had 300 of us. Maybe we can go to the principal also. So our batch, we had our 30 year anniversary. Somebody organized a dinner. To celebrate 30 years since our graduation. So we all came together. Everybody who wanted to come came, and that's how we reconnected with a much bigger group of girls."

"The nostalgia and the reminiscing comes after, much, much after. Really after growing up. Now we're old and then you feel it. So with your community now, with all of the girls you've reconnected with, do you talk about Emerald Hill often or do you talk about SCGS often? We talk about it all the time."

"So this over here, there used to be a huge tree. A huge tree. I don't know where it is but and this is where we played a lot of Zero Point. We did so much Zero Point here. We had Hopscotch here and all the kids and all the secondary schoolers, we all hang around here during recess. But there was a huge tree. All of us came here to do our science lesson. I don't know why. We came here to learn about the tree."

"I remember eating the kacang putih, the ice cream. Thank you. But where are they at, I cannot remember. Okay, so shall we head into the campus? Okay. I remember the two ice cream men at each corner. But I can't remember who is at which corner. But I remember one ice cream man's uncle, one ice cream uncle selling Magnolia ice cream, one uncle selling the durian ice cream, the Atachi ice cream, and they were at two different corners, so they don't affect each other's business."

"No, it's not shop, it's just like one uncle in motorbike, two uncles in motorbikes. Then with the ice cream cart beside him. So which was your favourite ice cream shop? I liked both. Like both? I will always frequent two uncles, to be fair. And you would go after school? Yeah, that is always after school. Yeah, we don't eat ice cream before school."

"I remember walking to school because I live nearby. I lived in Killeney Road when I was young. So actually I would walk to school through this... Through Emerald Hill? Yes, through this Emerald Hill. How was it to walk to school in the mornings? Hmm... It's okay. I mean, when I was young, I was accompanied by my mother, and when she goes on holiday, I'm accompanied by my grandmother. And that's... Oh wow. Oh wow, that's my worst memory. Oh no, wait, why? Because she would clutch onto my hand so tightly, I think I could feel the blood from me cut off."

"I remember the steps just now on my way here actually. Because I think, now that the thing is redeveloped, I'm generally walking... Because there's no more traffic, I will end up walking in the middle. But today I decided to walk along the five-foot way. And yeah, that kind of... I remember the steps and so on, and yes, I remember my grandmother. Dragging you. Almost cutting off my glasses."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): nostalgia (0.82), vivid recall (0.61), mild amusement (0.44), discomfort (0.38)
properties.emotion_all: Nostalgia, Vivid Recall, Mild Amusement, Discomfort

properties.Text: "Do you remember the mama store there? The Indian mama store down the road. They cut it off lah. Yeah, where they always sell those magazines, like fanfare during our time. And then we used to hang out there and buy all the gossip magazines."
"So this is your entrance every day. So you were dropped at school and you entered through here? Yeah, I entered through here."
"So when's the last time you both were here? Oh, during the alumni walk. Last year? Last year. During the alumni walk, we..."

"There was once we came by ourselves to walk around Orchard Road. And you entered? Yeah, at Merrill Hill. We didn't enter, we just walked past the school because it's not open to the public. So we just walked around at Merrill Hill Road. Around Merrill Hill Road to get the memories back."

"So in your time here, how did this entrance feel for you walking up? Hot... There's a canteen there, now I remember. It was very slopey to us. It's like a slope to us. When you were small? Yeah, when we were small, it's like a slope. A hike up. Yeah, a hike up to school."

"Yes! We have a friend whose grandmother was very kind. My mother can't be bothered with me. This friend's grandmother will queue and buy hot soup noodles, which we were too young to handle. And then she will wait for us at the canteen. She will buy everything and then we will just go and eat."
"And I remember the uncle, the famous SCGS mee ball. Oh yes. He's pretty famous. Yeah, he's pretty famous. Everybody remembers him. Was that your favourite lunch? Yes. That's my favourite lunch."
"Everything seems so small. So would you want the ceiling to be higher if they can change it now? Definitely."

"I remember the windows. Then we always hang out and buy the windows. There are the wooden windows that Kim Ju was saying. Do you remember any of this? A little. But I think we hang out along the steps here. We always hang out along the steps here and then play. I think we play the rubber band."

"Did you use the field often? Yes. For what? That's for the national day ceremony. Every national day. Every morning? No, national day celebration. Oh, national day. Would you perform? Every morning we would just stand here and say the pledge. Yeah, say the pledge. Yes. I remember national day parades here."
"No, no, no. They are definitely not there. So what was the view beyond the building? I think pretty much the sky, isn't it? Pretty much the sky. Wow."
"When you just walk into... It's so brown. Yeah. But it's not like that. I remember it's very open. When I entered, I could see the stationery uncle, Mr Chan there. Mr Chan. Yes."
"There's no feeling. No feeling? Just going to school? Just like going to school. To our primary school."
"I remember we all were wearing braces. Then you drop your braces off. Oh, God. I dropped my braces that was later but I think I dropped my tooth here."
"I wanted that classroom because it's a bit... This is the one. Same one. So you were in this class for sec 4? No. A primary 4."
"I remember the teacher, what's her name? Mrs Lee. She's very horrible. Horrible? Why horrible? Probably very fierce. Pull my ear and throw my exercise book on the floor."

"But that thing in today's standard I think is considered abuse. Yeah. But at this time the standard is not abuse. She complain to her mother, her mother say, Well, that's for not doing your homework."
"I think it's very easy. Easy? At my time, it's very hard. Yeah, I think it's easy when you were young too."
"That's where I had my brownie session. You were in the brownies? Yes, that's when the principal would make speech."
"It's just a hall for us. Just a hall for you. Yeah for us for school days activities."
"Now it's like maybe 50 people in your max and you feel very crowded already. When I was young I thought there's so many so many people and then the principal is down there giving an address."
"I think it should be. It should be. What if not then what become a residential? ... It needs life and it needs I think experiences that people want to come repeatedly otherwise it doesn't work."
"I remember Mr Ong. I don't want to go there and call out. Bad things. Mr Ong is one of the office clerk... There's this slim uncle. He's a stationary guy. He's like very fatherly one."
"We were underweight. And then we were given... Milk to drink. The terrible oat milk. You have to bring your own cup to drink... And then if don't put on weight. Then we will get into trouble."

"I think it has character even though it's small. To me, it's like a school. The view and the building structure. It's not like what the other schools now. It's very cold. It has a bit of like warm architecture of the building."
"Do you remember how it was to perform on stage? I think it's scary. Scary? Oh. Yeah. I think more scary than anything. Stage fright, I guess."
"Are you happy that this main building is going to be conserved? Yes. Very happy."

"I think I can visualise this place already. I think that one should become a wellness centre fully aircon because it's so dark and spooky so might as well have aircon. And then this one at least is more open and airy."

properties.Segment Text: "So I suppose before we go inside, this front of the building has been here very much like this for as long as I can remember. And the red concrete on top of this surface... Oh, yeah. So that has actually got squares marked in it. And I think a lot of us used to play hopscotch. Hopscotch. Up the top there, we would actually use chalk and draw on these red squares here and play hopscotch. And we had the big rubber band games at one stage. Yeah. And then the durian ice cream man would be just here. Just out here, like the ones that we have down in Ocho Roodner. There was a guy who was there. With a cart. Yeah, and he had been there since my mother was in school. Wow, that's amazing. So we used to wait here to get picked up after school, dropped off. Yeah, whereas we're waiting to get picked up."

"So I finished school here in 1980, after secondary four, and I actually went overseas. Yes, I was here from primary one all the way through to secondary four. And then left after I did my O-levels and went to Perth. And ended up not coming back because I met my husband in university there."

"So I remember this side entrance pretty well. It was usually open during school days. There were the two main gates there, but there was always this side entrance. I don't remember this being so open. But I remember these little drains going all around here. I think this has been concreted up since the school started, but there was no fencing on the field in those days. It was just open grass."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): nostalgia (0.84), loss / grief (0.48), mild disappointment (0.42), attachment / belonging (0.55)

properties.Segment Text: "The original school canteen was here, I think. From the start, the school canteen has always been in this location. So right at the entrance. And we had the Kacang Putih Man in there. And the drink seller with the blocks of ice. And we used to be able to go and chip ice off it to put in our plastic bags for drinks. And the mee pok guy was here as well. He would have the jar with all the crunchy bits and everything that you could go and help yourself to. I have a lot of memories of being here from the time I was quite young. And where you see these drains now, they're all closed. But they used to be open. And you remember the government used to run tooth brushing campaigns. And we would all squat along here and brush our teeth. Learn how to brush your teeth. And spit into the drain."

"I remember this complex being built, this one above. It wasn't always here. It was covered, but it was just single story because this was an actual canteen. And it wasn't concreted up or anything. And then eventually they built the gymnasium upstairs. So that hall upstairs has always been an assembly hall and performance area. So I do have photos of all of us when we were about 12. Primary 6 performing in concerts up the top here. The school has always had a very strong connection with music and dance. So from the days that I was here, we used to do a lot of that. There was choir and performances and singing. But even amongst the girls, I think we used to have year-end concerts. And we were all singing. And the funny thing, of course, now you think look back at it and think about what we were singing. We were all singing I Am Woman by Helen Brady, you know, up the top there. And so a whole bunch of very strong women, I think, came out of this school. Yes, still have all those friendships."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): pride (0.88), nostalgia (0.85), joy (0.74), empowerment (0.81), attachment / belonging (0.77)

properties.Segment Text: "This was the primary school in front of us here. We all started there. I know exactly where my classroom was. But I haven't been in these buildings for 40 years. The floor wasn't blue for a start. And of course, we had open windows. We had shutters and no air conditioning. And these floors have all been improved. They were all actually this material, but they were like a beige color. My first primary one classroom was either this one or this one. It was right here."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): nostalgia (0.89), loss / grief (0.46), longing (0.52), attachment / belonging (0.63)

properties.Segment Text: "The stairs used to be here, though. There were two quite grand staircases that went. They were wide staircases. And they went from this part of the building up to the floor. And it was double open. With no covering. It was just open. So they've obviously closed this in to use more of the space. But it was actually open. And you could go out through here. But this was a much bigger opening. And the bookshop was actually in there. So you bought your exercise books and your stationery and all those things in there. That's a shame they've lost the staircases, actually, because they were quite. It was quite a graceful design, rather than being so close up. They were beautiful. This whole area would have felt much more open."

"It was just open grass. And there were a whole line of travellers' palms down one end of this field. So all along that wall, that big wall, and they've concreted all of that up. But that was actually open and there were travellers' palms all across that wall. So it was green. It was green. You couldn't see through there. But they've clearly taken all the trees out. I don't know why they did that."

"We used to play rounders a lot. Just with a tennis ball and with your hand, nothing fancy. But we used to play on this ground here. And there was a huge tree here. There used to be a huge bunion tree. And there was a concrete seat that ran right around the tree that we would sit under and have lunch or recess because it was shady. It was very shady. We were, it was pretty open. There would be at least one or two games going on on either side of the field."

"Yeah, I do like the facade. I guess it's a bit sentimental. It's the original building. I think the windows, they put cheap aluminum windows in there. It doesn't look very nice. Well, at least something that looks nicer. I understand why they've done it, but it's not the nicest."

"When we did science projects and things, biology projects on ecosystems and all that sort of stuff, we would be out studying. I remember doing a project with a friend. And we found an ant's nest. So that was our science project. But it was down the bottom there."

"It was very residential. And of course, you didn't have this high-rise here either. It was all these old houses. I got dropped off every day at school and picked up. The cars would come in one way and pick you up and go off. They weren't allowed to come up here. This was teachers parking."

"The generational nature of it because teachers who taught her also taught me. And then also taught my sister. So we had this thing going where some of the teachers would call me by my mother's name. Then by the time they got my name, they would call my sister by my name. Of course, my sister was younger and I thought, you know, got a bit annoyed. Yeah, it was kind of nice to have the connection."
properties.Emotions (with confidence scores): attachment / belonging (0.81), nostalgia (0.77), amusement (0.59), mild annoyance (0.38)

properties.Segment Text: "My year was extremely interesting because I joined in 1964 But guess what? 1964 was where there was a confrontation And nobody could tell anyone much less a six or seven-year-old child what is the meaning of confrontation that when Malaysia became declared as a state So the story that the teachers told us on that day where emergency was declared was that today, tomorrow you don't have to come to school Why? Because there is curfew So the word curfew was given to us in a very happy tone of voice And we all just thought that it's so wonderful that we don't have to come to school because curfew to us meant a holiday And it meant that you mustn't come to school and you must stay at home So we had very different memories Only much later we realized that it was actually quite ominous to use the word curfew"
